_meta.description: EPSO AD/426/25 · Data Management · Seed v3 (194 MCQs, explanations with concept context). v2 150 MCQs kept verbatim; 44 MCQs appended covering DM-fundamentals gaps (collection, sources, sampling, validation, cleaning, emerging tech, EDA, ETL deep-dive, streaming, backup, DBMS basics, viz, text, time series).

mcqs.question: When did the General Data Protection Regulation (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) become applicable?

explanations.A: 27 Apr 2016 is the adoption date — the day the Council and Parliament signed off. Adoption is not the same as application: Art. 99 GDPR gave everyone a two-year grace period, so the rules only started applying two years later.
explanations.B: Correct. GDPR became applicable on 25 May 2018 (Art. 99), two years after adoption. The deliberate two-year gap gave companies, national DPAs and Member-State lawmakers time to prepare. From that date onward, the same rules apply in all 27 Member States.
explanations.C: 11 Dec 2018 is the application date of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 — the 'GDPR for EU institutions' (Commission, Parliament, agencies, EDPS itself). Different regulation, different scope.
explanations.D: 24 Sep 2023 is the application date of the Data Governance Act (DGA, Reg. 2022/868). A useful DGA fact to remember, but not what this question asks.

mcqs.question: Which regulation governs the processing of personal data by EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies?

options.A: Reg. (EU) 2016/679 — GDPR
options.B: Regulation (EU) 2018/1725
options.C: Dir. (EU) 2019/1024 — Open Data
options.D: Reg. (EU) 2022/868 — DGA

explanations.A: GDPR (Reg. 2016/679) explicitly excludes EU institutions from its material scope (Art. 2 GDPR). That carve-out is precisely why Regulation 2018/1725 had to exist — without it, the Commission, Parliament, ECB and agencies would have no personal-data rulebook at all.
explanations.B: Correct. Regulation 2018/1725 is the 'EU-institutions GDPR': same principles (lawful basis, data-subject rights, DPIA, DPO) but supervised by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) instead of national DPAs. If an exam question names an EU body — Commission, Parliament, Frontex, ECB — this regulation applies.
explanations.C: The Open Data Directive (2019/1024) concerns the reuse of public-sector information — it's a data-sharing rulebook, not a personal-data protection one.
explanations.D: The DGA (Reg. 2022/868) builds horizontal mechanisms for data reuse, intermediation and altruism on top of the existing personal-data regime. It doesn't replace the protection rules for EU bodies.

mcqs.question: Under the Open Data Directive (2019/1024), High-Value Datasets are defined in an Implementing Regulation with six thematic categories. Which of the following is NOT one of them?

options.A: Geo
options.B: Meteo
options.C: Health
options.D: Transit

explanations.A: Geospatial (abbreviated 'Geo') is one of the six HVD categories (Implementing Reg. 2023/138) — maps, cadastral data, addresses, administrative boundaries.
explanations.B: Meteorological (abbreviated 'Meteo') is one of the six categories — weather observations, forecasts, radar feeds, warnings.
explanations.C: Correct. Health is NOT an HVD category. Electronic health data is governed by the EHDS (Reg. 2025/327), the first operational European Data Space — a separate, sectoral regime. The six HVD categories are: geospatial · earth observation & environment · meteorological · statistics · companies & company ownership · mobility.
explanations.D: Mobility (labelled 'Transit' here) is one of the six categories — transport schedules, traffic data, charging-station locations, public-transport timetables.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four pillars of the Data Governance Act (Reg. (EU) 2022/868)?

options.A: Reuse of protected public-sector data
options.B: Data intermediation services
options.C: Data altruism
options.D: Cloud-to-cloud switching rights

explanations.A: Pillar 1 — reuse of protected public-sector data (health records, statistics, IP-protected, confidential commercial data). This extends Open Data Directive logic to data that can't be made 'open by default'.
explanations.B: Pillar 2 — data intermediation services. Neutral third parties that connect data holders and data users under a notification regime (Ch III of the DGA). They can't commercialise the data they broker.
explanations.C: Pillar 3 — data altruism for causes of general interest. Comes with a standardised European Data Altruism Consent Form (Ch IV) so donors can share data confidently, and a register of recognised altruism organisations.
explanations.D: Correct — the trap answer. Cloud / edge switching rights belong to the Data Act (Ch VI of Reg. 2023/2854), NOT the DGA. The DGA's fourth pillar is the European Data Innovation Board (EDIB) — the expert body that advises the Commission on data-spaces deployment and cross-sector interoperability.

mcqs.question: A manufacturer of connected coffee machines must comply with which chapter of the Data Act (Reg. (EU) 2023/2854) when the user asks to access the usage data the machine generates?

options.A: Chapter II — access to product data by users
options.B: Chapter III — B2B data sharing on FRAND terms
options.C: Chapter V — B2G in cases of exceptional need
options.D: Chapter VI — cloud and edge switching

explanations.A: Correct. Ch II of the Data Act gives the user of a connected product the right to access the data it generates. The user can also instruct the data holder to share this data with a third party of their choice — for example a repair shop, a competitor aftermarket service, or an analytics provider.
explanations.B: Ch III deals with B2B data sharing when a legal obligation to share exists — it sets FRAND (Fair, Reasonable And Non-Discriminatory) terms so the data holder can't impose abusive conditions. Not triggered by a consumer asking for their own usage data.
explanations.C: Ch V covers public-body access to private-sector data in situations of exceptional public need (public emergency, crisis). A user wanting their own data isn't a public emergency and isn't a public body.
explanations.D: Ch VI is about switching between cloud / edge providers — no ransom fees, functional equivalence, portability of workloads. Different stakeholders, different chapter, unrelated to IoT user-data access.

mcqs.question: Under the AI Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/1689), a real-time remote biometric identification system used in public spaces for law-enforcement purposes is categorised as:

options.A: Minimal risk — no mandatory obligations beyond voluntary codes
options.B: Limited risk — transparency obligations apply but no ban
options.C: High risk (subject to conformity assessment)
options.D: Unacceptable risk (banned, with narrow exceptions)

explanations.A: Minimal risk covers the bulk of AI systems — spam filters, recommendation engines, games — with voluntary codes of conduct and no mandatory obligations. Law-enforcement biometric ID is nowhere near this tier.
explanations.B: Limited risk triggers transparency duties — chatbots must disclose they are AI, deepfakes must be labelled. The concern is informed use, not safety. Biometric ID sits much higher on the pyramid.
explanations.C: Tempting because 'high risk' sounds severe, but it isn't severe enough. High-risk AI (Annex III) requires conformity assessment, documentation and human oversight — yet many biometric-ID uses still get more restrictive treatment. Real-time remote ID in public for police is the very top.
explanations.D: Correct. Art. 5 lists real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law-enforcement purposes as an 'unacceptable' practice, banned save narrowly defined exceptions (serious crime, prior judicial or independent administrative authorisation). This is the sharpest cut on the AI Act's risk pyramid.

mcqs.question: Article 10 of the AI Act imposes data-governance requirements on training, validation and test datasets of high-risk AI systems. Which obligation is specifically listed by Article 10?

options.A: Mandatory open-data publication of every training dataset used to build a high-risk AI system
options.B: Examination of possible biases and appropriate measures to detect, prevent and mitigate them
options.C: Exclusive use of synthetic data — no real-world observations allowed in training or test sets
options.D: Blanket prohibition of any personal data appearing in training, validation or test datasets

explanations.A: There is no blanket obligation to publish training datasets openly. Trade secrets, IP, personal data and security concerns all push against that. Art. 10 focuses on the quality of the datasets, not their public availability.
explanations.B: Correct. Art. 10(2)(f) requires examination of possible biases likely to affect health, safety or fundamental rights, with appropriate detection, prevention and mitigation measures. This is the heart of the Art. 10 data-governance obligations — alongside relevance, representativeness, error-minimisation and documented design choices.
explanations.C: Synthetic data (artificially generated) is explicitly allowed and can actually help satisfy bias-mitigation obligations when real data is scarce or skewed. But the AI Act doesn't require synthetic-only training — the key criteria are relevance and representativeness, whatever the source.
explanations.D: Personal data may be processed in training sets under specific safeguards. Art. 10(5) even allows processing of special categories of data (health, biometric, political opinions) where strictly necessary for bias monitoring and correction — a narrow derogation justified by the public interest in fair AI.

mcqs.question: The 'Data Union Strategy' announced in November 2025 is:

options.A: A binding regulation on Common European Data Spaces adopted by the Council
options.B: A directive implementing the Data Act
options.C: A Commission Communication (COM(2025) 835 final)
options.D: A joint statement from the EDPB

explanations.A: It's not a regulation — a regulation would be directly applicable EU law adopted by the Council and Parliament. 'Strategy' signals a policy document (a Communication), not binding law.
explanations.B: The Data Act is itself a regulation, directly applicable without national transposition. No implementing directive exists either, so no transposition is needed.
explanations.C: Correct. The Data Union Strategy is a Commission Communication, reference COM(2025) 835 final, published in November 2025. It's the successor policy framework to the 2020 European Strategy for Data, emphasising simplification and consolidation after five years of legislating (DGA, Data Act, AI Act, EHDS, Interoperable Europe Act).
explanations.D: The EDPB (European Data Protection Board) issues guidelines on GDPR and related personal-data matters. EU data-market strategies come from the Commission, not from a supervisory-body coordination committee.

mcqs.question: In the DAMA-DMBOK framework, which knowledge area is depicted at the centre of the wheel, connecting all others?

options.A: Data Architecture
options.B: Data Quality
options.C: Data Governance
options.D: Metadata

explanations.A: Data Architecture is one of the 11 DAMA knowledge areas but sits on the periphery of the wheel. Architecture decisions are shaped by governance policies, not the other way around.
explanations.B: Data Quality is a peripheral knowledge area — measured against standards set by governance, not setting the standards itself.
explanations.C: Correct. Data Governance is the central, cross-cutting knowledge area at the hub of the DAMA wheel. Every other area (architecture, quality, security, metadata, warehousing, master data, etc.) produces policies and KPIs that governance oversees, and is in turn shaped by the policies governance sets. That's why DAMA places it at the centre visually.
explanations.D: Metadata management is an important but peripheral knowledge area. Its outputs (catalogues, lineage) feed governance, but it isn't the hub.

mcqs.question: The FAIR principles for data stewardship stand for:

options.A: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
options.B: Fast, Aggregated, Integrated, Relational
options.C: Federated, Audited, Indexed, Referenced
options.D: Formal, Authoritative, Immutable, Reproducible

explanations.A: Correct. FAIR stands for Findable · Accessible · Interoperable · Reusable (Wilkinson et al., 2016). Originally proposed for scientific research data, now adopted as the default yardstick for open data across the EU — including data.europa.eu.
explanations.B: Not the FAIR definition. These are unrelated data-engineering adjectives strung together — be suspicious of plausible-sounding technical words glued into an acronym.
explanations.C: Plausible but wrong. 'Federated' (distributed architectures) and 'Audited' (security/governance) are real concepts, just not part of FAIR.
explanations.D: Reproducibility matters in research but isn't the FAIR wording. The fourth letter is Reusable — clear license, rich provenance, community standards — which enables reproducibility as a consequence.

mcqs.question: The European Interoperability Framework (EIF) defines four interoperability layers. Which is the correct order, from 'most policy' to 'most technical'?

options.A: Technical · Semantic · Organisational · Legal
options.B: Legal · Organisational · Semantic · Technical
options.C: Organisational · Semantic · Legal · Technical
options.D: Semantic · Technical · Legal · Organisational

explanations.A: Reversed. The EIF layers progress from policy down to technology, not the other way around. Reversing the order is a common distractor.
explanations.B: Correct. Legal · Organisational · Semantic · Technical. An Interoperability Governance overlay binds the four layers together, and 'Integrated Public Service Governance' sits on top as an overarching principle. The order matters: you can't have meaningful technical interoperability if the legal frameworks clash or organisations don't align on processes.
explanations.C: Legal precedes Organisational — legal alignment is the first layer because without compatible legal frameworks, nothing else can work.
explanations.D: Does not reflect the EIF structure at all. The layers are named and ordered specifically in the framework documentation.

mcqs.question: The CIA triad in information security refers to:

options.A: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
options.B: Compliance, Integrity, Auditability, Resilience
options.C: Control, Identification, Access
options.D: Consent, Isolation, Accountability

explanations.A: Correct. Confidentiality · Integrity · Availability — the three pillars of the CIA model, foundational for every major security control framework (ISO 27001, NIST CSF, etc.). Modern treatments often extend it with Authenticity and Non-repudiation.
explanations.B: 'Compliance, Integrity, Auditability, Resilience' looks close but swaps the real pillars. Auditability is a control practice (logs, traceability) that supports Integrity but isn't one of the three CIA objectives. Compliance is a goal, not a security pillar.
explanations.C: Control / Identification / Access sound like access-management concepts — relevant for designing RBAC/ABAC systems, but not what CIA stands for.
explanations.D: Consent is a GDPR lawful basis (Art. 6(1)(a)) — a privacy concept, not a security triad. Wrong domain entirely.

mcqs.question: Under the GDPR, a payroll service running employee salaries on behalf of a company is typically:

options.A: The data controller, because it physically handles the payroll salary data in its systems
options.B: The data processor, because it processes data on behalf of the controller
options.C: Jointly controller and processor by default under any outsourcing
options.D: Outside GDPR scope since pay slips are treated as public information

explanations.A: Controllership under GDPR is determined by who decides the purposes and means of processing — not by who physically handles the data. A processor's servers might store terabytes of personal data and still not be a controller.
explanations.B: Correct. The company determines the purposes (pay our employees) and the essential means (use this payroll service). The payroll provider acts on the company's instructions, making it a processor under Art. 4(8). A data processing agreement (Art. 28) must cover the relationship, including instructions, security, sub-processors and deletion at the end of the service.
explanations.C: Joint controllership (Art. 26) is a specific legal construct requiring joint determination of purposes and means — typical for platforms where two entities genuinely co-design the processing (e.g. social-media page admins + the platform). It isn't the default for a service-provider relationship.
explanations.D: Pay slips contain personal data of employees (name, salary, social-security data) and are in GDPR scope regardless of who physically sees them. 'Public' applies to anonymous statistics, not individual pay data.

mcqs.question: What is DCAT-AP primarily used for on the EU data.europa.eu portal?

options.A: A machine-learning model registry for EU-funded AI projects and systems
options.B: An application profile of the DCAT vocabulary to describe datasets and catalogues
options.C: A file format used for personal-data export requests submitted under GDPR Art. 20
options.D: A public directory of SaaS cloud providers available to EU administrations

explanations.A: DCAT-AP is not a model registry — no EU standard under this name concerns ML model metadata. Don't confuse with AI-Act-era model-card ideas, which are different and newer.
explanations.B: Correct. DCAT-AP (Data Catalogue Vocabulary — Application Profile) is an EU-specific profile of the W3C DCAT vocabulary, used to describe datasets and catalogues consistently across European open-data portals. It's what powers data.europa.eu. Key extensions include StatDCAT-AP (statistics) and GeoDCAT-AP (geospatial), with a dedicated HVD profile for High-Value Datasets.
explanations.C: GDPR data portability (Art. 20) requires common structured / machine-readable formats (CSV, JSON, XML) but doesn't mandate DCAT-AP specifically. DCAT-AP is about cataloguing datasets, not about exporting personal data.
explanations.D: DCAT-AP describes datasets and catalogues on data portals, not cloud SaaS providers. Cloud-provider directories are a different (and much less standardised) category.

mcqs.question: A data team adopts ELT instead of ETL when ingesting logs into a modern cloud warehouse. The key difference is that in ELT:

options.A: Transformations happen before loading, inside a dedicated ETL engine
options.B: Transformations happen inside the target store after loading
options.C: No transformations are applied to the data at any stage
options.D: Data is loaded into storage but never queried downstream

explanations.A: That describes the classic ETL pattern (Extract → Transform → Load): the on-premise workflow where transformations run in a dedicated ETL engine (Informatica, SSIS, Talend) before the data lands in the target.
explanations.B: Correct. In ELT (Extract → Load → Transform), raw data is loaded straight into the target — typically a modern columnar warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) — and transformations run there using SQL. This works because the warehouse's compute is cheap and elastic, removing the need for a separate ETL server. Modern stacks like dbt are built around this pattern.
explanations.C: ELT still transforms — the letters just rearrange when. 'No transformations' would be replication / change-data-capture, not ELT.
explanations.D: Loading data without ever querying it is archival or cold storage, not ELT. ELT specifically exists to make data queryable fast.

mcqs.question: Which statement about the GDPR treatment of anonymised vs pseudonymised data is correct?

options.A: Both anonymised and pseudonymised data fall outside GDPR scope once applied
options.B: Pseudonymised data falls outside GDPR; anonymised data stays in scope
options.C: Anonymised data falls outside GDPR; pseudonymised data remains in scope
options.D: Both are always in GDPR scope regardless of technique applied

explanations.A: Only true anonymisation removes data from GDPR scope. Pseudonymised data, because it can be re-identified through the key, remains personal data and stays in scope.
explanations.B: Reversed. It's anonymisation that takes data out of scope — pseudonymisation is a safeguard, not an exemption. This swap is one of the exam's favourite traps.
explanations.C: Correct. Anonymisation (irreversible — no re-identification possible even when combined with other data sets) means the data is no longer personal and therefore outside GDPR (Recital 26). Pseudonymisation (reversible via a separate key kept secure) is explicitly defined in Art. 4(5) as a safeguard — the data remains personal and GDPR still applies. The key mental test: can someone with access to additional information re-identify the person? If yes, it's pseudonymisation.
explanations.D: Properly executed anonymisation is explicitly recognised in Recital 26 as taking data out of scope — otherwise the concept would be meaningless. The challenge is that 'properly executed' is hard to achieve in practice (linkage attacks, auxiliary data).

mcqs.question: An analytics team needs ACID transactions, schema enforcement and time-travel directly on object storage holding raw Parquet files, while still supporting BI dashboards and ML feature pipelines. Which architecture best fits?

options.A: A classic proprietary data warehouse with nightly batch-ingestion pipelines
options.B: A Hadoop-era data lake relying on schema-on-read only
options.C: A lakehouse using an open table format such as Delta, Iceberg or Hudi
options.D: A relational OLTP database with read replicas as the back-end

explanations.A: A classic data warehouse uses proprietary storage (Snowflake's micro-partitions, BigQuery's Capacitor, Redshift blocks) — it doesn't operate directly on user-managed object storage with open Parquet files. It also wouldn't give you time-travel out of the box unless the vendor adds it.
explanations.B: A plain Hadoop-era lake lacks ACID transactions, lacks schema enforcement, and has no time-travel. Schema-on-read alone is fine for exploration, but BI tools expect structured, consistent tables and concurrent reads/writes break.
explanations.C: Correct. A lakehouse layers an open table format — Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi — on top of object-store files (typically Parquet). This adds the warehouse-like guarantees (ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time-travel, efficient upserts) while keeping the lake's openness and low cost. Both BI tools and ML pipelines can read the same tables, eliminating the classic 'copy data to the ML cluster' step.
explanations.D: OLTP databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) with read replicas are optimised for transactional workloads — row-oriented storage, small short transactions. Analytics scans over large Parquet files would be painfully slow, and you'd lose the object-storage cost advantage entirely.

mcqs.question: Which statement best distinguishes a KPI from an ordinary metric?

options.A: A KPI must always be expressed as a percentage of some fixed reference total value baseline
options.B: A KPI is a metric chosen for its strategic link to an objective and used to track progress
options.C: A KPI is only valid if refreshed in real time from streaming source systems
options.D: A KPI and a dashboard are just interchangeable labels for the same monitoring artefact

explanations.A: KPIs can take many forms — counts (users onboarded), ratios (conversion rate), currency (revenue), percentages (uptime). Format doesn't define whether something is a KPI.
explanations.B: Correct. A KPI (Key Performance Indicator) is a metric that has been deliberately selected because it ties to an organisational objective. 'Key' means someone thought about it and decided this metric tells us whether we're making progress. Without that strategic link, it's just a metric. Good KPIs are SMART — specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound.
explanations.C: Refresh cadence depends on the business context — many important KPIs are tracked monthly, quarterly or even annually (customer satisfaction, market share). Real-time is a nice-to-have, not a defining requirement.
explanations.D: A KPI and a dashboard are not the same thing. A dashboard is the visualisation layer that may display KPIs alongside other charts and filters. They're complementary, not synonymous.

mcqs.question: In a supervised machine-learning pipeline for fraud detection, 'feature engineering' refers to which activity?

options.A: Assigning the 'fraud / not fraud' ground-truth label to each historical transaction record in the dataset
options.B: Creating predictive variables (e.g. rolling-average amount, device-country mismatch flag) from raw data
options.C: Evaluating the trained model's predictive performance on a held-out test set of unseen transactions
options.D: Defining role-based access controls that govern which analysts can read the model's training data

explanations.A: That describes data labelling — assigning the target class (fraud / not fraud) to historical examples, typically done manually, via weak-labelling heuristics, or by using a trusted source of truth. Labels are an input to supervised learning; features are the other input.
explanations.B: Correct. Feature engineering is the craft of designing and deriving the input variables the model will learn from — things like 'transactions in the last 24 h', 'device-country mismatch flag', 'log-amount', or embeddings of categorical fields. It's where domain knowledge turns into predictive signal, and in practice it often has more impact on model performance than the choice of algorithm.
explanations.C: Evaluating a model on a held-out test set is model validation / evaluation — measuring how well the model generalises to unseen data. Feature engineering happens upstream; evaluation happens downstream.
explanations.D: RBAC governs who can access the training data — a security / compliance concern. It's completely disjoint from the data-science task of deriving useful input variables.

mcqs.question: Which of the following correctly pairs EU multilingual reference resources with their primary purpose?

options.A: IATE — a controlled thesaurus used to index EU publications; EuroVoc — the EU inter-institutional terminology database
options.B: EuroVoc — the EU inter-institutional terminology database; IATE — a multilingual thesaurus for EU documents
options.C: IATE — the EU's inter-institutional terminology database; EuroVoc — a multilingual thesaurus for indexing EU documents
options.D: IATE — a public-procurement catalogue for EU tenders; EuroVoc — the EU translation memory used by translators

explanations.A: Swaps the roles. IATE is not a thesaurus — it's the EU's inter-institutional terminology database.
explanations.B: Swaps the roles. EuroVoc is not a terminology database — it's the EU's multilingual thesaurus.
explanations.C: Correct. IATE (Inter-Active Terminology for Europe) is the EU's inter-institutional terminology database — concepts defined across 24 official languages, maintained collaboratively by translators and terminologists in all EU institutions. EuroVoc is the multilingual thesaurus used to index documents across institutions, structured according to SKOS and covering all EU policy fields. Different tools for different jobs: IATE for terms (what does this word mean?), EuroVoc for classification (how do I file this document?).
explanations.D: Neither resource is a procurement catalogue. The EU translation memory is Euramis, not EuroVoc. Public-procurement data lives in TED (Tenders Electronic Daily), a separate portal.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six lawful bases for processing listed in Art. 6(1) GDPR?

options.A: Consent of the data subject
options.B: Legitimate interest of the controller
options.C: Commercial profitability of the controller
options.D: Performance of a contract with the data subject

explanations.A: Consent (Art. 6(1)(a)) is one of the six bases — it must be freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous (Art. 7), and withdrawable at any time.
explanations.B: Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) is one of the six bases, available where the controller's or a third party's interest is not overridden by the data subject's rights. It requires a documented balancing test.
explanations.C: Correct. Commercial profitability is not a GDPR lawful basis. The six bases are: consent · contract · legal obligation · vital interests · public task · legitimate interest. Profitability might motivate legitimate-interest claims, but the legal basis itself has to be one of the named six.
explanations.D: Performance of a contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) is a standard basis — e.g. shipping an order you purchased requires processing your address.

mcqs.question: Under Art. 33 GDPR, within what timeframe must a controller notify the competent supervisory authority of a personal data breach?

options.A: Immediately, without any defined window
options.B: Within 24 hours of becoming aware
options.C: Within 72 hours of becoming aware, where feasible
options.D: Within 30 days, aligned with the DPIA consultation period

explanations.A: GDPR does not use a vague 'immediately' standard for breach notification — it sets a clock (72 hours) precisely so controllers have a workable, auditable deadline.
explanations.B: 24 hours is the e-Privacy / telecom breach-notification rule for electronic-communication providers, not the GDPR standard.
explanations.C: Correct. Art. 33(1) requires notification to the supervisory authority without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after becoming aware of the breach. If later, reasons for the delay must accompany the notification. Notification to affected data subjects (Art. 34) is separately required when the breach is likely to result in a high risk to their rights and freedoms.
explanations.D: 30 days is unrelated to breach notification. Prior-consultation deadlines with the DPA for high-risk DPIAs are different (and longer), governed by Art. 36.

mcqs.question: The GDPR right to data portability (Art. 20) applies specifically when processing is based on:

options.A: Any of the six lawful bases in Article 6 of the GDPR
options.B: Consent or a contract, and is carried out by automated means
options.C: Legitimate interest, exclusively, with no other basis admitted
options.D: A legal obligation imposed on the controller by Union or Member State law

explanations.A: Portability has a narrower scope than the other data-subject rights. It is not triggered by every lawful basis.
explanations.B: Correct. Art. 20(1) limits portability to processing (a) based on consent (Art. 6(1)(a) or Art. 9(2)(a)) or on a contract (Art. 6(1)(b)), AND (b) carried out by automated means. The output must be a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format. Paper records and public-task processing are out of scope.
explanations.C: Legitimate interest is explicitly excluded — portability is limited to consent / contract bases.
explanations.D: Legal-obligation processing (e.g. tax records a company must keep) does not trigger portability. Access (Art. 15) still applies, but not portability.

mcqs.question: Which body supervises the application of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 in EU institutions?

options.A: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB)
options.B: The Court of Justice of the European Union
options.C: The national Data Protection Authority of the host Member State
options.D: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS)

explanations.A: The EDPB coordinates national DPAs on GDPR matters. The EDPS is an EDPB member but not the enforcement authority for EU institutions — the EDPS role is separate.
explanations.B: The CJEU adjudicates cases brought before it. It is not the day-to-day supervisor — that role is executive, and belongs to the EDPS.
explanations.C: National DPAs supervise GDPR within their Member State. They have no jurisdiction over EU institutions — those operate under the separate 2018/1725 regime.
explanations.D: Correct. The EDPS (European Data Protection Supervisor) is the independent authority that supervises EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies on personal-data matters under Reg. 2018/1725. It can investigate complaints, conduct audits, issue reprimands and impose fines on EU bodies.

mcqs.question: The Open Data Directive (EU) 2019/1024 primarily governs:

options.A: The protection of personal data of natural persons in the Union
options.B: Cross-border electronic identification and mutual recognition
options.C: Mandatory data-sharing obligations imposed on private companies in the EU
options.D: Reuse of public-sector information and publicly funded research data

explanations.A: Personal-data protection is the domain of GDPR (Member States) and Reg. 2018/1725 (EU institutions). The Open Data Directive is not a privacy instrument.
explanations.B: Cross-border eID is governed by eIDAS (Reg. 910/2014, revised by 2024/1183). Unrelated to open-data reuse.
explanations.C: Private-to-private data-sharing is the Data Act's space (Ch III FRAND terms). The Open Data Directive focuses on the public sector.
explanations.D: Correct. The Open Data Directive replaces the 2003 PSI Directive and extends open-by-default principles to both public-sector information and publicly funded research data. Key ideas: free of charge, machine-readable formats, API access where feasible, and a dedicated High-Value Datasets regime (Implementing Reg. 2023/138).

mcqs.question: Under Chapter III of the DGA (2022/868), a recognised data intermediation service is NOT allowed to:

options.A: Use the data it intermediates for its own commercial purposes
options.B: Provide technical infrastructure for data exchange between holders and users
options.C: Be notified to the competent authority of its Member State
options.D: Facilitate the exercise of data-subject rights on behalf of individuals

explanations.A: Correct. A central design choice of Ch III: the intermediary must be strictly neutral. It cannot commercialise the data it routes (e.g. by selling it on, enriching its own products, or cross-selling). Neutrality is what distinguishes DGA intermediaries from ordinary data brokers and earns user trust. Breach of this principle voids the service's recognition.
explanations.B: Providing technical infrastructure is exactly what an intermediation service does — the DGA builds a trusted pipe between holders and users.
explanations.C: Notification to the competent authority is mandatory under Art. 11 DGA — it's how the service acquires its recognised status and is listed publicly.
explanations.D: Intermediation for data subjects exercising their rights (e.g. portability agents, consent-management services) is explicitly contemplated as a legitimate intermediation category.

mcqs.question: The European Data Altruism Consent Form introduced by the DGA (Ch IV) is designed to:

options.A: Replace the standard GDPR consent requirements for all processing of personal data
options.B: Serve as an intra-institutional authorisation form used within the Commission services
options.C: Standardise how individuals and companies donate data for causes of general interest
options.D: Operate as a commercial licence template for paid data transactions between firms

explanations.A: Data altruism does not override GDPR. The altruism consent form is an additional standardised mechanism that still sits on top of the GDPR lawful-basis framework.
explanations.B: Nothing in the altruism regime is internal to the Commission — the mechanism is outward-facing and available EU-wide.
explanations.C: Correct. The European Data Altruism Consent Form (Art. 22 DGA, with a Commission implementing act setting its layout) is a common template so donors understand what they are agreeing to when contributing data for objectives of general interest — health research, climate science, combating emergencies, official statistics. Recognised altruism organisations must be not-for-profit and listed in a public register.
explanations.D: Altruism is explicitly non-commercial. The form is a donation mechanism, not a licence for paid transactions — those sit under other instruments (Open Data Dir., Data Act Ch III).

mcqs.question: When did the Data Governance Act (Reg. (EU) 2022/868) become applicable?

options.B: 17 July 2021
options.C: 24 September 2023
options.D: 12 September 2025

explanations.A: 25 May 2018 is the GDPR application date.
explanations.B: 17 July 2021 is the Open Data Directive transposition deadline.
explanations.C: Correct. The DGA was adopted 30 May 2022 and became applicable on 24 September 2023, after a 15-month grace period. Memorise this alongside the other EU data-regulation dates — sequencing questions are common.
explanations.D: 12 September 2025 is the Data Act application date, not the DGA.

mcqs.question: Chapter VI of the Data Act (Reg. 2023/2854) addresses:

options.A: Biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces
options.B: Reuse of protected public-sector data held by bodies
options.C: Switching between cloud or edge data-processing services
options.D: Data altruism and the standardised European consent form

explanations.A: Biometric identification is the AI Act's turf (Art. 5 bans, Annex III high-risk).
explanations.B: Protected public-sector data reuse is DGA Chapter II, not Data Act.
explanations.C: Correct. Ch VI of the Data Act sets rules on switching between providers of cloud / edge services: no contractual, technical or financial barriers, phased removal of switching / egress fees (target: zero by 2027) and functional equivalence requirements so a customer can actually move workloads. It's the piece of the Data Act aimed at breaking vendor lock-in.
explanations.D: Data altruism sits in DGA Chapter IV. The Data Act has no altruism chapter.

mcqs.question: A public body requests access to proprietary data held by a private company to respond to a major flood. Which Data Act mechanism applies?

options.A: Chapter II — user access to connected-product data
options.B: Chapter III — B2B FRAND sharing when a legal obligation exists
options.C: Chapter V — B2G access in cases of exceptional need
options.D: Chapter VI — cloud and edge switching

explanations.A: Ch II is about the user of a connected product receiving their own data, not public authorities obtaining company data for crisis response.
explanations.B: Ch III governs B2B data sharing when another act already imposes a sharing obligation. It sets FRAND commercial terms — unrelated to emergency public access.
explanations.C: Correct. Ch V ('Business to Government' — B2G) lets public bodies obtain private-sector data in situations of exceptional need: responding to a public emergency (floods, wildfires, pandemics) or preventing / mitigating one. The Act limits scope, ties the request to necessity and proportionality, and forbids using the data for unrelated purposes.
explanations.D: Ch VI concerns cloud customer switching. Different problem entirely.

mcqs.question: Under Ch III of the Data Act, a data holder that is legally obliged to share data with a data recipient must offer terms that are:

options.A: Fair, Reasonable And Non-Discriminatory (FRAND)
options.B: Identical to those of the holder's most favoured customer
options.C: Free of charge in all circumstances
options.D: Subject to bilateral confidentiality with no remuneration limits

explanations.A: Correct. Ch III (Arts. 8–12) requires terms that are Fair, Reasonable And Non-Discriminatory. FRAND lets data holders recover reasonable costs and, for non-SME recipients, a reasonable margin — while preventing abusive, excessive or exclusionary pricing. FRAND is borrowed from standard-essential-patent law and adapted to data.
explanations.B: 'Most favoured customer' is a benchmark in some commercial agreements but is not the Data Act standard. FRAND is a balance, not a match-the-best-deal rule.
explanations.C: Free-of-charge is the Open Data Directive logic for public-sector information, not B2B private-sector sharing under the Data Act.
explanations.D: FRAND explicitly limits remuneration to what is reasonable. Unlimited pricing with NDAs would defeat the chapter's purpose.

mcqs.question: Under the AI Act, a chatbot deployed by a retailer to answer customer questions typically falls into which risk tier?

options.A: Unacceptable risk — the system is prohibited outright with no market access allowed
options.B: High risk — subject to a full conformity assessment before being placed on the market
options.C: Limited risk — transparency obligation (user must know they are interacting with AI)
options.D: Minimal risk — no obligation applies and no transparency notice needs to be shown

explanations.A: Prohibited AI is the narrow list in Art. 5 (social scoring, real-time remote biometric ID for police, manipulative practices). A retail chatbot is nowhere near.
explanations.B: High-risk AI (Annex III) covers sensitive domains — recruiting, credit scoring, education, migration, critical infrastructure, justice. A shop chatbot is not in scope of Annex III by default.
explanations.C: Correct. Conversational AI systems fall under the 'limited-risk / transparency' category. The operator must ensure users are aware they are interacting with an AI system (unless it is obvious from context). This is the core transparency obligation of Art. 50.
explanations.D: Minimal risk covers systems with no specific obligation (spam filters, games). Chatbots sit one step above because of the transparency duty.

mcqs.question: Under Chapter V of the AI Act, General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models with 'systemic risk' are subject to:

options.A: Only a light-touch voluntary code of conduct, similar to ordinary GPAI obligations
options.B: Full Annex III high-risk obligations applied by default to every systemic-risk foundation GPAI model on the market
options.C: Specific obligations including model evaluations, systemic-risk assessment and cybersecurity protection
options.D: A blanket prohibition on deployment of systemic-risk GPAI anywhere in the Union

explanations.A: Ordinary GPAI gets transparency, copyright and technical-documentation duties. Systemic-risk GPAI has stricter obligations than a code of conduct — the point of the tier is to impose binding duties on the largest models.
explanations.B: GPAI is governed by its own chapter (Ch V), not by the Annex III high-risk regime. High-risk obligations apply where a GPAI model is integrated into a high-risk AI system, not to the foundation model by default.
explanations.C: Correct. Art. 55 imposes additional obligations on GPAI with systemic risk: state-of-the-art model evaluations (including adversarial testing), assessment and mitigation of systemic risks at Union level, incident reporting, and adequate cybersecurity of the model and its infrastructure. The tier is triggered by capability thresholds (cumulative training compute > 10^25 FLOPs) or Commission designation.
explanations.D: GPAI is not banned. The AI Act regulates it, staggered by capability and risk — prohibition is reserved for Art. 5 practices.

mcqs.question: The Interoperable Europe Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/903) primarily targets interoperability of:

options.A: Private-sector B2B data exchanges across the single market
options.B: Consumer IoT devices sold within the EU internal market
options.C: Internal Commission HR systems and service-wide intranets
options.D: Cross-border public services delivered in or by the Union

explanations.A: Private-sector B2B exchanges are governed by the Data Act (Ch III) and data-space initiatives, not the Interoperable Europe Act.
explanations.B: IoT devices are covered by the Data Act (Ch II), Cyber Resilience Act and product-safety legislation — not the Interoperable Europe Act.
explanations.C: The scope is EU-wide public administrations, not just the Commission's internal systems.
explanations.D: Correct. The Interoperable Europe Act creates a framework for cross-border interoperability of public services in the Union. Key tools: mandatory interoperability assessments (Art. 3) before major public-sector IT spend, the Interoperable Europe Board (MS + Commission), a portal of reusable building blocks (eDelivery, eSignature, SEMIC) and GovTech / regulatory-sandbox support.

mcqs.question: Art. 3 of the Interoperable Europe Act introduces a mandatory 'interoperability assessment'. It must be carried out:

options.A: Before Union entities or public-sector bodies make binding decisions on new or substantially modified trans-European IT solutions
options.B: After a new cross-border public service has already been deployed and gone live across Member States, once operational feedback has been collected
options.C: Only for private-sector software procurements exceeding €10 million in contract value
options.D: Only on IT systems that process special categories of personal data under GDPR Art. 9

explanations.A: Correct. Art. 3 requires Union entities and public-sector bodies to carry out an interoperability assessment before binding decisions on trans-European digital public services, to identify cross-border effects and plan around the EIF layers. The assessment feeds the Interoperable Europe Portal, creating a searchable library for reuse.
explanations.B: Post-deployment assessment would defeat the purpose — you can't un-spend money on an incompatible system. The assessment is ex ante, specifically to avoid building incompatible systems in the first place.
explanations.C: There is no €10 million threshold — the obligation applies to trans-European public-service IT regardless of procurement value.
explanations.D: Special-category personal data triggers a GDPR DPIA (Art. 35), not an interoperability assessment. Different instruments, different focus.

mcqs.question: Under the European Health Data Space Regulation (EU) 2025/327, researchers wishing to use electronic health data for secondary purposes must:

options.A: Publish the data on the Open Data Portal free of charge
options.B: Register as a DGA data altruism organisation
options.C: Obtain a Data Act FRAND licence from the hospital
options.D: Apply via a recognised Health Data Access Body (HDAB)

explanations.A: Health data is not open-by-default — it's sensitive personal data under GDPR Art. 9 and must be handled under the EHDS secondary-use regime, not the Open Data Directive.
explanations.B: Data altruism (DGA Ch IV) is a separate mechanism for voluntary donations of data for general-interest causes. Not the EHDS secondary-use route.
explanations.C: Data Act Ch III FRAND terms apply to B2B data sharing when a separate obligation to share exists — not to health research. Hospitals don't licence patient data to researchers bilaterally under EHDS.
explanations.D: Correct. The EHDS channels secondary uses (research, innovation, policy, regulatory activities) through Health Data Access Bodies (HDABs). These Member-State bodies vet applications, pair purposes with appropriate data, apply minimisation and pseudonymisation, and provide the data in secure processing environments. HDABs are the template the other European Data Spaces are expected to follow.

mcqs.question: The 'European Strategy for Data' (Feb 2020, COM(2020) 66) is best described as:

options.A: A binding EU regulation directly applicable across all Member States without national transposition
options.B: A Council conclusion adopted on cybersecurity policy priorities
options.C: An EDPB opinion on cross-border processing of personal data
options.D: A Commission Communication setting out a vision for a single market for data

explanations.A: Regulations are binding legal acts directly applicable without transposition. The 2020 Strategy is not a regulation — the documents it inspired (DGA, Data Act, AI Act) are.
explanations.B: Council conclusions are another instrument; the 2020 Strategy came from the Commission, not the Council.
explanations.C: EDPB opinions focus on personal-data questions, not on broad data-market strategy.
explanations.D: Correct. The European Strategy for Data is a Commission Communication (policy document, non-binding) published in February 2020. It articulated the vision of a single market for data and a programme of Common European Data Spaces. The 2025 Data Union Strategy (COM(2025) 835) is its update.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 11 DAMA-DMBOK knowledge areas?

options.A: Data Quality
options.B: Data Warehousing and Business Intelligence
options.C: Data Monetisation
options.D: Metadata Management

explanations.A: Data Quality is one of the 11 areas — measuring and improving fitness-for-use against defined criteria.
explanations.B: Data Warehousing and Business Intelligence is an area in its own right in DAMA-DMBOK.
explanations.C: Correct. 'Data Monetisation' is not a DAMA-DMBOK knowledge area. The 11 areas are: Data Governance (hub), Data Architecture, Data Modelling & Design, Data Storage & Operations, Data Security, Data Integration & Interoperability, Document & Content Management, Reference & Master Data, Data Warehousing & BI, Metadata, Data Quality.
explanations.D: Metadata Management is one of the 11 peripheral areas of the DAMA wheel.

mcqs.question: In the FAIR principles, 'Accessible' means specifically that data:

options.A: Are retrievable via a standardised, open communication protocol that allows authentication / authorisation where needed
options.B: Must always be freely and openly available to anyone on the internet without any authentication
options.C: Must always be physically stored on servers located inside the European Union and its territory
options.D: Must always be made available in at least the 24 official EU languages at the point of initial publication and subsequent updates

explanations.A: Correct. Accessible (A1: via a standardised protocol; A1.1 free, open, universally implementable; A1.2 allows authentication / authorisation; A2: metadata is accessible even when the data are no longer available). The emphasis is on machine-retrievability and persistence of metadata, not on zero-cost open access.
explanations.B: FAIR accessibility does not require open-to-all access. It requires a standardised protocol for retrieval, which can include authentication and authorisation. Protected data can still be FAIR.
explanations.C: FAIR is storage-location neutral. Sovereignty is a separate concern addressed by other instruments (e.g. GDPR transfers, EUCS).
explanations.D: Multilingualism matters at EU level but isn't part of the FAIR definition — FAIR is a scientific-data framework, not an EU-language policy.

mcqs.question: On top of the four EIF layers, what additional element binds them together and coordinates cross-layer decisions?

options.A: DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment)
options.B: Architectural Ref.
options.C: Common Procur. Voc.
options.D: Interoperability Governance

explanations.A: A DPIA is a GDPR (Art. 35) obligation for high-risk personal-data processing. It has no structural role in the EIF.
explanations.B: There is no separate 'Architectural Reference Model' in EIF — the EIF itself is the reference model.
explanations.C: The CPV (Common Procurement Vocabulary) is a classification for public procurement notices. Unrelated to EIF structure.
explanations.D: Correct. The EIF explicitly adds an 'Interoperability Governance' overlay across the four layers (Legal, Organisational, Semantic, Technical), plus 'Integrated Public Service Governance' as an overarching principle for jointly designing public services. Without governance, decisions at each layer drift apart.

mcqs.question: SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System) is primarily used to:

options.A: Encrypt RDF triples at rest in a triple-store backend
options.B: Represent thesauri, taxonomies and classification schemes on the Semantic Web
options.C: Query relational database tables using the standard SQL syntax across vendors
options.D: Define ACID transaction boundaries in cloud lakehouses

explanations.A: SKOS is a modelling vocabulary, not an encryption scheme. RDF at rest is typically protected by storage-level or transport-level encryption, not by SKOS.
explanations.B: Correct. SKOS is a W3C recommendation for representing thesauri, taxonomies and subject-heading systems as RDF data, so that vocabularies like EuroVoc can be published on the Semantic Web with consistent structure (concept, prefLabel, altLabel, broader, narrower, related).
explanations.C: SQL queries relational tables across vendors. SPARQL — not SKOS — is the query language for RDF graphs.
explanations.D: ACID in lakehouses is provided by open table formats (Delta, Iceberg, Hudi). SKOS has nothing to do with transaction semantics.

mcqs.question: Which statement about the Semantic Web stack is correct?

options.A: OWL is the query language for RDF graphs; SPARQL defines class hierarchies and property restrictions on top of RDF triples
options.B: RDF is a relational database management system; OWL is a physical storage format for triples
options.C: SPARQL is an ontology formalism equivalent to OWL; RDF is a transport format for ontologies
options.D: RDF represents facts as subject-predicate-object triples; SPARQL is the query language; OWL is an ontology language

explanations.A: OWL is not a query language — SPARQL is. OWL adds formal semantics (classes, properties, restrictions) on top of RDF to support reasoning.
explanations.B: RDF is a data model for the Semantic Web, not a DBMS. Triple stores (Virtuoso, GraphDB, Jena) implement RDF storage; RDF itself is the model.
explanations.C: Wrong — those are different roles. SPARQL retrieves, OWL models meaning.
explanations.D: Correct. RDF (Resource Description Framework) expresses facts as subject-predicate-object triples using URIs. SPARQL is the W3C query language for triple stores. OWL (Web Ontology Language) builds on RDF Schema to let you define classes, properties, cardinality and other formal constraints that reasoners can use.

mcqs.question: Tim Berners-Lee's 5-star Open Data scheme awards the top rating (★★★★★) to data that:

options.A: Is published as a PDF document with an openly available Creative Commons licence
options.B: Uses non-proprietary file formats such as CSV or JSON for structured data
options.C: Uses URIs to identify things and is linked to other data (Linked Open Data)
options.D: Is available through a paywalled commercial API protected by OAuth authentication

explanations.A: PDF + open licence is only ★ — on the web, openly licensed. It is not machine-readable in any structural sense.
explanations.B: Non-proprietary formats (CSV, JSON) earn ★★★, not ★★★★★.
explanations.C: Correct. The ladder: ★ on web, open licence → ★★ machine-readable structured data → ★★★ non-proprietary format → ★★★★ use URIs to identify things → ★★★★★ link to other data (Linked Open Data). The top tier enables discovery and cross-dataset queries across the web.
explanations.D: A paywalled API is not open by any definition. The scheme starts from 'openly licensed' as a minimum; paywalls exclude a dataset entirely.

mcqs.question: In data-management role models, the Data Owner is distinguished from the Data Steward because the Owner:

options.A: Physically stores and backs up the data in an IT operations capacity
options.B: Is accountable for the data domain and sets policy for its use
options.C: Writes the SQL queries used to extract the data
options.D: Acts as the GDPR data subject whose personal data is being processed

explanations.A: Physical storage and backup fall to the Data Custodian — typically the IT operations team.
explanations.B: Correct. The Data Owner is a senior business stakeholder accountable for a data domain (customers, products, suppliers). They decide who may use the data, approve quality thresholds and sign off on definitions. The Steward executes the policy day-to-day — enforcing quality rules, maintaining definitions, resolving issues.
explanations.C: Writing queries is an analyst / engineer activity, not an Owner's role.
explanations.D: The data subject is the individual whose personal data is processed (GDPR Art. 4(1)). Not a data-management role at all.

mcqs.question: Which statement correctly contrasts the Chief Data Officer (CDO) with the Data Protection Officer (DPO)?

options.A: CDO and DPO are synonymous roles in the EU institutions and perform exactly the same tasks day to day
options.B: CDO is a role mandated by GDPR; DPO is a voluntary strategic function defined only by Commission practice
options.C: CDO is an organisation-level strategic data-leadership role; DPO is a GDPR / 2018/1725 compliance role independent from management
options.D: The CDO reports directly to the DPO in every GDPR-regulated entity by default in order to ensure the strategic compliance alignment

explanations.A: They are different roles with different mandates and reporting lines. Combining them is a governance anti-pattern because it blurs strategic and compliance objectives.
explanations.B: The opposite is true. The DPO is the GDPR-mandated role (Arts. 37–39 GDPR, and 2018/1725 for EU institutions). The CDO is a strategic business role with no legal definition.
explanations.C: Correct. The CDO (Chief Data Officer) owns the organisation's overall data strategy, governance programme and value creation. The DPO is a legally required role for public authorities and certain processing (Art. 37 GDPR), must operate independently, advise on compliance, and report to the highest level of management. Distinct mandates, distinct independence requirements.
explanations.D: No such reporting line exists. In fact the DPO must not receive instructions on carrying out their compliance tasks (Art. 38(3) GDPR).

mcqs.question: Which ISO standard lists 15 inherent and system-dependent characteristics of data quality (accuracy, completeness, consistency, credibility, currentness, …)?

options.A: ISO 9001
options.B: ISO 8000
options.C: ISO/IEC 25012
options.D: ISO/IEC 27001

explanations.A: ISO 9001 is the general quality-management-system standard — not specific to data.
explanations.B: ISO 8000 deals with data quality, with a strong focus on master-data exchange and provenance. It does not contain the 15-characteristic list.
explanations.C: Correct. ISO/IEC 25012 (part of the SQuaRE 25000 series) defines 15 data-quality characteristics split between inherent (accuracy, completeness, consistency, credibility, currentness) and system-dependent (accessibility, compliance, confidentiality, efficiency, precision, traceability, understandability, availability, portability, recoverability).
explanations.D: ISO/IEC 27001 is the information-security management standard. CIA triad, controls, ISMS — not quality characteristics.

mcqs.question: ISO 8000 on data quality is particularly associated with:

options.A: Cybersecurity incident response and CSIRT coordination
options.B: Continuous-integration pipeline testing and DevOps / DevSecOps tooling
options.C: Multilingual machine-translation evaluation metrics
options.D: Master-data exchange, provenance and syntactic / semantic encoding

explanations.A: Cybersecurity incident response is the domain of ISO/IEC 27035 and equivalents — not ISO 8000.
explanations.B: CI pipelines are tested using software-engineering and DevOps practices, not ISO data-quality standards.
explanations.C: Machine-translation evaluation has its own metrics (BLEU, chrF) — nothing to do with ISO 8000.
explanations.D: Correct. ISO 8000 is oriented toward master-data exchange: it specifies how data is encoded (syntactic quality), how it matches an agreed vocabulary (semantic quality), and how its provenance is recorded. Parts of the standard cover portable master data, data provenance, and quality measurement for master data.

mcqs.question: Which of the following best describes a logical data model?

options.A: Tablespaces, index types and partitioning chosen for a specific DBMS product release
options.B: The encryption-at-rest configuration applied to a storage bucket by default
options.C: The UML deployment diagram of a production cluster with its nodes and links
options.D: A technology-neutral representation of entities, attributes and relationships

explanations.A: Those are physical-model concerns — choices tied to a specific DBMS product release. The logical model precedes physical implementation.
explanations.B: Encryption-at-rest is a data-security control, not a modelling concept.
explanations.C: Deployment diagrams belong in infrastructure architecture, not data modelling.
explanations.D: Correct. A logical model captures what the data means — entities, attributes, relationships, cardinalities — independently of the DBMS chosen. It sits between the conceptual (very high level, business terms only) and the physical (DBMS-specific structures, indexes, storage). Separating the three is a classical Zachman / DAMA practice.

mcqs.question: A system supporting thousands of short, concurrent write transactions per second for an e-banking application is best classified as:

options.A: OLAP — online analytical processing
options.B: Data mart for quarterly reporting
options.C: HDFS — Hadoop distributed file system
options.D: OLTP — online transaction processing

explanations.A: OLAP is optimised for long, read-heavy analytical queries over historical data — columnar storage, star schemas. Concurrent small writes would be painful.
explanations.B: A data mart is a subject-oriented slice of a warehouse, used for reporting — read-heavy, not write-heavy.
explanations.C: HDFS is a batch-oriented file system for large-scale analytics, not a transactional database.
explanations.D: Correct. OLTP systems (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server) are built for many short, ACID transactions — row-oriented storage, tight locking, low-latency commits. E-banking, e-commerce checkout, reservation systems are textbook OLTP workloads.

mcqs.question: Two EU public administrations exchange documents where 'customer' means different things in each context. Which EIF layer must they reconcile to achieve semantic interoperability?

options.A: Legal
options.B: Organisational
options.C: Semantic
options.D: Technical

explanations.A: Legal alignment addresses whether the exchange is lawful — compatible bases, transfer rules. It doesn't settle the meaning of 'customer'.
explanations.B: Organisational alignment harmonises processes and responsibilities. It helps with 'who does what' but not with 'what the word means'.
explanations.C: Correct. The Semantic layer of EIF covers meaning: aligning vocabularies, code lists and definitions so that 'customer' in system A refers to the same concept in system B. Tools include SKOS thesauri, Core Vocabularies (Core Person, Core Business, Core Location) and DCAT-AP.
explanations.D: Technical alignment covers protocols, formats and APIs — how bytes move, not what they mean.

mcqs.question: In a 'data mesh' architecture, which of the following is a defining principle?

options.A: A single centralised team owns all enterprise data as the sole authoritative source
options.B: All enterprise data must be stored in one monolithic warehouse
options.C: Domain-oriented decentralised ownership with data treated as a product
options.D: No data governance is applied at all; teams freely choose any tool or store they want

explanations.A: Centralised ownership is the antipattern the mesh tries to fix. A central platform team creates bottlenecks; domains can't scale.
explanations.B: A single warehouse is orthogonal to — and often at odds with — mesh thinking. Mesh is about distributed ownership, not a single store.
explanations.C: Correct. Data mesh (Zhamak Dehghani, 2019) rests on four principles: domain-oriented decentralised ownership, data-as-a-product, self-serve data platform, and federated computational governance. Each domain owns and publishes its data products with clear SLAs, documentation and contracts, while a shared platform and global policies keep interoperability and compliance intact.
explanations.D: Mesh explicitly requires federated computational governance. 'No governance' is not mesh; it's chaos.

mcqs.question: EU 'Core Vocabularies' (Core Person, Core Business, Core Location, Core Public Service) are maintained by the SEMIC initiative to:

options.A: Provide a minimum set of reusable, simplified, extensible data models for cross-border public services
options.B: Serve as the legal basis for cross-border transfers of personal data under GDPR Chapter V
options.C: Replace the DCAT-AP application profile used on the data.europa.eu portal for dataset metadata descriptions
options.D: Act as the official EU translation memory for administrative and legal texts across languages

explanations.A: Correct. SEMIC's Core Vocabularies are minimal, reusable data models for key public-sector concepts — Person, Business, Location, Public Service. They reduce semantic drift across administrations and are referenced by the Interoperable Europe Act ecosystem of reusable building blocks.
explanations.B: Legal bases for transfers come from GDPR Chapter V (adequacy, SCCs, BCRs) — not vocabularies.
explanations.C: DCAT-AP and Core Vocabularies are complementary: DCAT-AP describes datasets; Core Vocabularies describe the real-world entities those datasets refer to.
explanations.D: The EU translation memory is Euramis — nothing to do with SEMIC Core Vocabularies.

mcqs.question: In relational design, the third normal form (3NF) aims to eliminate:

options.A: Primary keys on normalised tables
options.B: Any form of indexing applied physically to the relational tables
options.C: Foreign-key integrity constraints
options.D: Transitive dependencies between non-key attributes

explanations.A: Primary keys are essential to normalisation, not the thing being eliminated. A table without a primary key isn't even in 1NF in the modern reading.
explanations.B: Indexing is a physical optimisation, orthogonal to normalisation.
explanations.C: Foreign keys enforce referential integrity between normalised tables; they support normalisation rather than contradict it.
explanations.D: Correct. 3NF requires the table to be in 2NF and that every non-key attribute depend on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key (Codd's pithy summary). Transitive dependencies — where a non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute — are removed by splitting the table.

mcqs.question: A dimensional-modelling team chooses a 'star schema' over a 'snowflake schema' primarily because:

options.A: Star schemas normalise their dimension tables to reduce redundant storage overhead
options.B: Snowflake schemas are only legal for government-sector data workloads
options.C: Star schemas keep dimensions denormalised, making BI queries simpler and faster
options.D: Snowflake is strictly a file format, not a schema-design pattern at all

explanations.A: It's the opposite. Snowflake schemas normalise dimensions into multiple related tables to reduce redundant storage. Stars keep them flat.
explanations.B: Nothing in data law constrains schema design to stars or snowflakes.
explanations.C: Correct. A star schema has a central fact table surrounded by denormalised dimension tables — simpler joins, faster BI queries, easier for business users to understand. Snowflake schemas normalise dimensions (e.g. splitting 'Product → Category → Department'), saving storage but adding joins that slow typical reporting queries.
explanations.D: Snowflake is the name of both a dimensional design pattern and a cloud data warehouse product. The question is about the schema pattern.

mcqs.question: In an event-driven architecture using Apache Kafka, a consumer 'replays' events by:

options.A: Deleting the existing topic and re-producing all events from the original source
options.B: Resetting its offset on the relevant partitions to an earlier position
options.C: Requesting a fresh stream from the cluster administrator
options.D: Creating a brand-new broker in the Kafka cluster to host the replay traffic

explanations.A: Deleting and re-producing is destructive and not how replay works. Kafka retains events for a configurable period; replay is just a matter of reading old offsets again.
explanations.B: Correct. Kafka stores events in append-only partitioned logs and tracks each consumer's position as an 'offset'. To replay, a consumer commits (or seeks to) an earlier offset and re-reads the log. This makes Kafka ideal for event sourcing and for reprocessing after a bug fix — no source system needs to be touched.
explanations.C: There is no 'request a new stream' operation — consumers pull directly from partitions via offsets.
explanations.D: Adding brokers scales capacity; it has nothing to do with replay.

mcqs.question: Under the CAP theorem, in the presence of a network partition a distributed system must choose between:

options.A: Cost and Performance
options.B: Compression and Parallelisation
options.C: Clarity and Portability
options.D: Consistency and Availability

explanations.A: CAP concerns correctness and responsiveness trade-offs, not cost optimisation.
explanations.B: Compression and parallelisation are performance techniques, not the CAP trade-off.
explanations.C: Not CAP letters — these sound plausible but don't correspond.
explanations.D: Correct. Brewer's CAP theorem states that a distributed data system can provide at most two of Consistency, Availability and Partition-tolerance. Because partitions (network failures) are a fact of life, real designs must choose C or A during a partition. CP systems prefer correctness (reject writes / reads that might be stale); AP systems prefer responsiveness (serve possibly stale data).

mcqs.question: A 'reference architecture' for a data platform is primarily:

options.A: A fully deployed, running production system already serving users in real time
options.B: A single vendor's proprietary product catalogue of licensed tools and services
options.C: A standard set of components, patterns and principles to guide the design of concrete systems
options.D: A detailed per-cloud-region cost estimate used to build a financial business case for the deployment

explanations.A: Reference architectures describe the template; deployed systems are instances built from the template.
explanations.B: A vendor catalogue maps onto an architecture; it isn't one.
explanations.C: Correct. A reference architecture is a blueprint — named components (ingest, storage, processing, serving, catalogue, governance), interaction patterns, technology-agnostic principles. Teams use it to avoid reinventing the wheel and to keep designs consistent across projects. The EU Big-Data Value Reference Model and DAMA's architecture area are good examples.
explanations.D: Cost estimates support a business case for a deployment. They aren't the architectural design.

mcqs.question: Which activity best exemplifies data governance as defined by DAMA-DMBOK?

options.A: Writing SQL transformations in dbt
options.B: Defining who is accountable for a data domain and under which policies it may be used
options.C: Configuring a Kafka cluster for high throughput
options.D: Optimising a neural-network hyperparameter

explanations.A: Writing transformations is data engineering, not governance. Governance sets the rules that engineering follows.
explanations.B: Correct. DAMA defines data governance as the exercise of authority, control and shared decision-making over the management of data assets. Accountability for domains (Owner / Steward / Custodian), policies, standards and dispute resolution sit here. It's the hub of the DAMA wheel because every other area both contributes to and is constrained by governance.
explanations.C: Cluster tuning is operations / engineering. It follows governance's SLA and retention requirements but isn't governance itself.
explanations.D: Hyperparameter tuning is ML / data science — governed by policy but not a governance activity.

mcqs.question: A governance team assesses its maturity using the DAMA-DMBOK / CMMI-DM scale. A level associated with 'defined processes documented and consistently used across the enterprise' is best described as:

options.A: Initial / ad hoc
options.B: Repeatable but undocumented
options.C: Defined (standardised and institutionalised)
options.D: Optimising (continuous improvement with measurement)

explanations.A: Initial / ad hoc means processes exist only in individuals' heads — not documented, not consistent. That's level 1.
explanations.B: Repeatable implies processes recur but are not formally documented or enterprise-wide. That's level 2.
explanations.C: Correct. At the 'Defined' level (commonly level 3), processes are standardised, documented and institutionalised across the enterprise. Everyone follows the same playbook even if metrics aren't yet tracked.
explanations.D: Optimising (level 5) adds quantitative management and continuous improvement on top of 'Managed' (level 4). The question describes documented + consistent, which is one step below.

mcqs.question: A 'Data Steward' in a governance programme is typically responsible for:

options.A: Approving budgets for data platform investments
options.B: Deciding whether GDPR applies to the organisation
options.C: Backing up and restoring data in production
options.D: Executing data-quality rules, maintaining business definitions and resolving data issues in a domain

explanations.A: Budget approval is typically a CDO / executive sponsor responsibility, not a Steward's.
explanations.B: Whether GDPR applies is a legal question, answered by the DPO / legal counsel based on material and territorial scope.
explanations.C: Backup and restore are Custodian / IT-operations tasks.
explanations.D: Correct. Stewards are the operational arm of governance: they curate business glossary entries, enforce data-quality thresholds, triage data issues reported by consumers, and coordinate with Owners when policy changes are needed. Without Stewards, policies stay on paper.

mcqs.question: Under Art. 38 GDPR (and mirrored in Reg. 2018/1725), the DPO must:

options.A: Report to the line manager for daily tasks but not on compliance
options.B: Be independent and report to the highest management level without receiving instructions on tasks
options.C: Be a member of the executive board
options.D: Hold Power of Attorney for all controller decisions

explanations.A: Compliance is precisely the area where the DPO must be independent — hence the 'no instructions' rule of Art. 38(3).
explanations.B: Correct. Art. 38(3) GDPR requires that the DPO does not receive any instructions regarding the performance of their tasks, cannot be dismissed or penalised for performing them, and reports directly to the highest management level. The same architecture applies to EU institutions under Art. 44 of Reg. 2018/1725.
explanations.C: The DPO is not required to be on the executive board — only to have direct access to it.
explanations.D: The DPO advises and monitors; they do not sign off on behalf of the controller. Decisional authority stays with the controller.

mcqs.question: Art. 30 GDPR (and Art. 31 Reg. 2018/1725) require maintaining 'Records of Processing Activities' (RoPA). For controllers, these records must include:

options.A: Purposes of processing, categories of data subjects and data, recipients, transfers to third countries where applicable, retention periods and a general description of security measures
options.B: Only the names of data subjects who consented
options.C: The exact SQL queries used in the controller's databases
options.D: A daily log of every read operation on personal data

explanations.A: Correct. Art. 30(1) GDPR enumerates what a controller's RoPA must contain: purposes; categories of subjects and data; categories of recipients including in third countries; where applicable, identification of transfers and the safeguards; envisaged time limits; and a general description of technical and organisational security measures under Art. 32. It's the backbone of the accountability principle (Art. 5(2)).
explanations.B: Names of data subjects are personal data in themselves; a RoPA is about processing operations, not a list of consenting individuals.
explanations.C: SQL queries are operational artefacts, not RoPA content. Records are conceptual descriptions at the processing-activity level.
explanations.D: A per-read audit log is an optional security control, not a GDPR RoPA requirement. RoPA sits at a higher level of abstraction.

mcqs.question: A business glossary in a data-governance programme primarily provides:

options.A: Physical table definitions and index types
options.B: A list of all data subjects with open GDPR requests
options.C: The encryption keys used for at-rest storage
options.D: Agreed business definitions of terms and KPIs across the organisation

explanations.A: Physical definitions belong in a data dictionary / catalogue at the technical level — not in the business glossary.
explanations.B: DSAR tracking is handled by a rights-management / compliance tool, not a glossary.
explanations.C: Key management belongs in security / HSM infrastructure, not in a glossary.
explanations.D: Correct. The business glossary records agreed definitions for terms used by the business — 'customer', 'active user', 'churn rate', 'net revenue' — and their owners. It is the semantic source-of-truth that dashboards, reports and contracts refer back to, preventing the 'same word, different number' problem.

mcqs.question: The GDPR principle of 'accountability' (Art. 5(2)) requires the controller to:

options.A: Be able to demonstrate compliance with the data-protection principles
options.B: Pay administrative fines before processing begins
options.C: Publish all processing activities publicly
options.D: Report every processing activity to the Court of Justice

explanations.A: Correct. Art. 5(2) adds 'accountability' on top of the six substantive principles (lawfulness/fairness/transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity/confidentiality). Controllers must not only comply — they must be able to demonstrate compliance, e.g. through RoPA, DPIAs, policies, training records, audit trails.
explanations.B: Fines are enforcement actions, not a precondition for processing.
explanations.C: Publicity is not required — RoPA is internal (though DPAs can request it). Privacy notices (Arts. 13–14) are the public-facing transparency instrument.
explanations.D: The CJEU does not receive operational reports from controllers.

mcqs.question: Two EU agencies jointly determine the purposes and means of a shared processing activity. They are:

options.A: Controller and processor
options.B: Joint controllers, with their arrangement spelled out in a transparent agreement
options.C: Outside the scope of Reg. 2018/1725
options.D: Automatically subject to GDPR instead of Reg. 2018/1725

explanations.A: Controller + processor requires one entity to instruct the other. Joint determination of purposes and means is structurally different.
explanations.B: Correct. Where two or more controllers jointly determine purposes and means, they are joint controllers (Art. 26 GDPR; Art. 28 Reg. 2018/1725). Their respective responsibilities — especially regarding data-subject rights and transparency — must be set out in an arrangement, with the essence made available to data subjects.
