The CJEU has applied this broadly (Fashion ID, Jehovah's Witnesses, Wirtschaftsakademie).
explanations.C: Processing by EU bodies falls under Reg. 2018/1725 regardless of whether it is solo or joint.
explanations.D: GDPR doesn't apply to EU institutions — Art. 2 GDPR carves them out. 2018/1725 is the applicable regulation whether control is solo or joint.

mcqs.question: Under Art. 25 GDPR ('Data protection by design and by default'), the controller must implement:

options.A: Appropriate technical and organisational measures at the time of determining the means of processing and at the time of processing itself
options.B: Encryption of all personal data in all circumstances
options.C: A DPO regardless of scale, complexity or risk
options.D: Open publication of all processing code

explanations.A: Correct. Art. 25(1) sets two moments: when determining the means (design time) and during processing (run time). The measures must be appropriate to the state of the art, costs, nature/scope/context/purposes of processing and risks. Art. 25(2) adds 'by default': only data necessary for each specific purpose may be processed.
explanations.B: Encryption is one possible measure but not legally mandatory in all cases — it must be 'appropriate' to the risk.
explanations.C: DPO appointment is governed by Art. 37, with specific triggers (public authority; core activities involving regular and systematic monitoring on a large scale; large-scale processing of special categories). Not unconditional.
explanations.D: Open-source publication is not a GDPR obligation; it may even conflict with security requirements.

mcqs.question: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) was established to:

options.A: Supervise EU institutions instead of the EDPS
options.B: Ensure consistent application of GDPR across Member States, issue guidelines and decide binding disputes between national DPAs
options.C: Draft EU data-market strategies
options.D: Adjudicate criminal cases related to data breaches

explanations.A: The EDPS supervises EU institutions under Reg. 2018/1725. The EDPB coordinates national DPAs under GDPR.
explanations.B: Correct. Under GDPR Chapter VII, the EDPB is composed of the heads of each national DPA plus the EDPS. It issues guidelines, recommendations and best practices, and decides binding disputes under the one-stop-shop consistency mechanism (Art. 65). Its guidelines, while not legally binding, are highly authoritative in practice.
explanations.C: Strategy documents come from the Commission, not the EDPB.
explanations.D: Criminal adjudication is for Member-State courts (and, via preliminary rulings, the CJEU). The EDPB is an administrative body.

mcqs.question: Which of the following best represents the stages of a classical data lifecycle?

options.A: Prediction → Visualisation → Compression → Deletion
options.B: Ingest → Cache → Display → Log
options.C: Train → Validate → Deploy → Monitor
options.D: Create / acquire → Store → Use → Archive → Destroy

explanations.A: Those are ML / visualisation steps, not a data-lifecycle model.
explanations.B: Those are application / observability terms, not the canonical lifecycle stages.
explanations.C: That describes an ML model lifecycle, not a data lifecycle.
explanations.D: Correct. A standard lifecycle moves through acquisition (creation / ingestion from sources), storage, use (analysis, integration, sharing), archival (reduced-access retention), and destruction (secure deletion once retention ends). Policy attaches retention rules, quality rules and access rules to each stage. Storage-limitation (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e)) makes the end of the lifecycle legally binding for personal data.

mcqs.question: The 14 Common European Data Spaces announced in the 2020 European Strategy for Data are:

options.A: Regulations directly applicable to all Member States
options.B: Private blockchain networks
options.C: A single cloud service operated by the Commission
options.D: Sectoral initiatives to pool and share data within specific domains (health, agriculture, energy, mobility …)

explanations.A: Only some data spaces are accompanied by binding regulations (EHDS for health). Others progress through pilots, implementing acts and sector-specific initiatives.
explanations.B: Data spaces are technology-neutral. Some pilots use blockchain; most do not.
explanations.C: Not a single service — they are networks of participants using shared standards and governance, not a monolithic cloud.
explanations.D: Correct. The 14 Common European Data Spaces cover Agriculture, Cultural heritage, Energy, Finance, Green Deal, Health (EHDS — first operational), Industry & manufacturing, Language, Media, Mobility, Public administration, Research & innovation, Skills, and Tourism. Each pools sector-specific data under common governance, technical building blocks (Simpl) and support (DSSC — Data Spaces Support Centre).

mcqs.question: A public administration categorises datasets as 'public · internal · confidential · restricted'. This practice is called:

options.A: Data normalisation
options.B: Data anonymisation
options.C: Data lineage
options.D: Data classification

explanations.A: Normalisation is a schema-design technique (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF). Unrelated to sensitivity tiers.
explanations.B: Anonymisation is an irreversible transformation of personal data — a technique, not a classification scheme.
explanations.C: Lineage traces data from origin through transformations to destinations. Different concept.
explanations.D: Correct. Data classification assigns each dataset to a sensitivity tier that determines handling — who can access it, encryption requirements, retention, permitted export. It is a prerequisite for proportionate security controls and a recurring requirement in ISO 27001, EU RESTREINT / CONFIDENTIEL rules, and public-sector information-security policies.

mcqs.question: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) ('storage limitation') requires that personal data be kept:

options.A: Indefinitely, to ensure future auditability
options.B: For no longer than necessary for the purposes for which they are processed
options.C: For exactly seven years regardless of purpose
options.D: Forever if the controller obtained one-time consent

explanations.A: Indefinite retention contradicts storage limitation. Audit retention must itself have a defined period justified by a legal basis.
explanations.B: Correct. Storage limitation is one of the six substantive GDPR principles: personal data kept in a form permitting identification must be retained only for as long as necessary for the purposes. Longer storage is allowed only for archiving in the public interest, scientific/historical research or statistical purposes, subject to appropriate safeguards (Art. 89).
explanations.C: Seven years is a common national bookkeeping rule — not a GDPR rule. Each purpose demands its own assessment.
explanations.D: Consent does not override retention limits. The purpose still bounds how long data can be kept.

mcqs.question: The Data Act (Art. 32) addresses international governmental access to non-personal data held in the EU. Its core rule is that cloud / data-processing providers must:

options.A: Always comply with any foreign court order
options.B: Notify the European Commission before each routine customer request
options.C: Store all data exclusively in a single Member State
options.D: Prevent or challenge third-country governmental access where it would conflict with Union or Member-State law

explanations.A: Automatic compliance with any foreign order would ignore the EU legal framework and the Art. 32 safeguards.
explanations.B: No Commission pre-notification is required for ordinary customer requests; Art. 32 concerns foreign-government access specifically.
explanations.C: The Data Act does not impose single-Member-State localisation. EU data may be stored and processed across the Union and beyond, subject to safeguards.
explanations.D: Correct. Art. 32 of the Data Act requires providers of data-processing services to take 'all adequate technical, organisational and legal measures' to prevent international governmental access to non-personal data that would conflict with Union or Member-State law. Compliance with foreign court or administrative orders is only possible where an international agreement exists, or under narrowly defined conditions.

mcqs.question: The Data Spaces Support Centre (DSSC) is:

options.A: A regulatory body with enforcement powers
options.B: An EU-funded initiative providing technical, legal and organisational guidance to accelerate Common European Data Spaces
options.C: A national certification body under the NIS2 Directive
options.D: A private data broker recognised under the DGA

explanations.A: The DSSC has no enforcement powers — enforcement under data-space-related law sits with regulators (DPAs, EDPS, national competent authorities, the Commission).
explanations.B: Correct. The DSSC is a Horizon-Europe-funded initiative whose mission is to support the roll-out of Common European Data Spaces — producing blueprints, reusable building blocks, governance templates, and convening stakeholders across sectors. It complements Simpl (the open-source middleware) and the data.europa.eu portal.
explanations.C: NIS2 certification bodies are national and focused on cybersecurity, not data spaces.
explanations.D: DGA intermediaries are notified private services; the DSSC is an EU-supported coordination initiative, not a broker.

mcqs.question: Which statement best distinguishes a data strategy from a data policy?

options.A: A strategy sets direction and priorities; a policy translates direction into binding rules for specific activities
options.B: They are synonyms
options.C: A strategy is signed by the DPO; a policy by the CDO
options.D: A policy is a multi-year plan; a strategy is a one-page rule

explanations.A: Correct. A data strategy articulates vision, objectives, priorities, investments and roadmap for data over multiple years. A data policy is a binding rule on a specific topic (retention, classification, sharing, quality) that employees and systems must follow. Strategies inform which policies are needed; policies operationalise the strategy.
explanations.B: They play different roles in the governance stack.
explanations.C: Neither sign-off is fixed by role. Strategies are typically owned by the CDO / executive; policies often by domain Owners with DPO advisory input on personal data.
explanations.D: Reversed and wrong in both directions. Strategies tend to be longer-horizon documents; policies are the operational rules.

mcqs.question: 'Simpl', referenced in EU data-policy documents, is:

options.A: A cryptocurrency launched by the Commission
options.B: A national registry of data intermediaries
options.C: A directive replacing the DGA
options.D: An open-source smart middleware platform to federate EU data spaces and cloud-to-edge resources

explanations.A: Simpl has nothing to do with crypto or CBDC.
explanations.B: National intermediary registries fall under the DGA — not Simpl.
explanations.C: Nothing replaces the DGA; it is in force. Simpl is technical infrastructure, not a legal act.
explanations.D: Correct. Simpl is an open-source, smart, federated middleware being developed by the Commission to interconnect participants in the Common European Data Spaces and enable cloud-to-edge data processing. It provides shared building blocks — identity, authorisation, interoperable connectors — that data spaces can plug into rather than each reinventing them.

mcqs.question: A data-space design must deliver 'federated computational governance'. This concept primarily means:

options.A: Running all governance processes on a single shared database
options.B: Delegating all governance to the Commission
options.C: Global rules encoded as code and enforced automatically across distributed domain data products, while leaving domain-specific decisions to domain owners
options.D: Removing all policies so that domains are free to decide

explanations.A: Federation is the opposite of centralising everything in one database.
explanations.B: Delegation to the Commission would centralise authority and defeat the federation design.
explanations.C: Correct. Federated computational governance (a mesh principle that data spaces adopt) means global policies — interoperability, security, compliance, access — are defined once and enforced in code across distributed domain data products, while domain owners retain autonomy over domain-specific rules. The federated governance body typically includes representatives from each domain to ratify global policies.
explanations.D: No policies would eliminate trust and interoperability — data spaces would not function.

mcqs.question: The 'Digital Decade' policy programme aims to achieve EU digital targets by:

explanations.A: 2026 is close — but that's not the Digital Decade horizon.
explanations.B: 2050 is the Union's climate-neutrality target, not the digital programme.
explanations.C: 2040 is a climate horizon, not a digital one.
explanations.D: Correct. The 2030 Digital Decade Policy Programme (Decision (EU) 2022/2481) sets quantified targets for 2030 in four axes: skills, infrastructure, business digitalisation, and digitalisation of public services. Member States and the Commission track progress through annual reports and coordinated roadmaps. It is the overarching frame for EU digital policy (including data and AI).

mcqs.question: In a REST API exposing a list of employees, the HTTP verb used to retrieve an employee by ID is:

options.A: POST
options.B: PATCH
options.C: DELETE
options.D: GET

explanations.A: POST is used to create resources or submit data that doesn't naturally fit a specific resource URI.
explanations.B: PATCH applies a partial update. It's for modification, not retrieval.
explanations.C: DELETE removes the resource. It would not be used to retrieve.
explanations.D: Correct. REST maps CRUD to HTTP verbs: GET retrieves (safe, idempotent), POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH updates partially, DELETE removes. Fetching /employees/42 is a textbook GET.

mcqs.question: Which is the defining characteristic of stream processing versus batch processing?

options.A: Stream processing operates on unbounded data in near-real time while batch operates on bounded, periodically scheduled data sets
options.B: Stream processing operates on bounded, scheduled data sets while batch operates on unbounded data
options.C: Stream processing always uses SQL; batch never does
options.D: Batch processing is always faster than stream processing

explanations.A: Correct. Stream processing treats data as an unbounded sequence of events arriving continuously, processed near-real-time with low latency (Kafka Streams, Flink, Spark Structured Streaming). Batch processes a bounded dataset collected over a window (hour, day) with higher latency but often simpler semantics. Modern engines unify both under the same API (e.g. Flink's unified batch/stream model).
explanations.B: Reversed — the definitions are swapped.
explanations.C: Both can use SQL — see Flink SQL, Spark SQL, ksqlDB.
explanations.D: Speed depends on metrics (throughput vs latency). Streaming typically has lower latency; batch can have higher throughput.

mcqs.question: In Apache Kafka, partitions within a topic provide:

options.A: A mechanism to shard the topic for parallelism and to preserve ordering within each partition
options.B: Automatic encryption-at-rest for each consumer
options.C: A built-in schema registry
options.D: Exactly-once delivery to every consumer by default

explanations.A: Correct. Partitions are the unit of parallelism: consumers in a consumer group share partitions so throughput scales. Order is guaranteed within a partition (not across partitions). Producers typically choose partitions via a key hash so messages for the same key land in the same partition and keep order.
explanations.B: Encryption-at-rest is a storage-level concern, configured outside Kafka's partition semantics.
explanations.C: The schema registry is a separate component (Confluent Schema Registry, Apicurio) and not tied to partitions.
explanations.D: Exactly-once is available but requires transactional producers and careful consumer configuration — it's not the default.

mcqs.question: Apache Airflow represents a scheduled data-processing workflow as a:

options.A: Cyclic finite-state machine
options.B: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of tasks with dependencies
options.C: Single monolithic SQL script
options.D: Recursive tree of monadic transformations

explanations.A: Cycles would prevent deterministic scheduling. Airflow's graph is explicitly acyclic.
explanations.B: Correct. Airflow models a workflow as a DAG: nodes are tasks (extract, transform, load, publish), edges are dependencies (task B runs after A succeeds). The scheduler runs tasks when their upstream dependencies are met, with retries, SLAs, and backfills. Alternatives include Prefect, Dagster, Argo Workflows.
explanations.C: A DAG can include SQL tasks but isn't itself SQL.
explanations.D: Not an Airflow concept. Airflow's model is the DAG — pragmatic, not monadic.

mcqs.question: Compared to REST, a GraphQL API typically lets the client:

options.A: Receive push notifications when data changes
options.B: Ask for exactly the fields it needs in a single request, avoiding over- or under-fetching
options.C: Skip authentication by default
options.D: Commit database transactions on the server

explanations.A: Real-time push is provided by subscriptions, WebSockets or Server-Sent Events — supported in GraphQL but not unique to it.
explanations.B: Correct. GraphQL exposes a single endpoint and a strongly typed schema. Clients compose queries that specify exactly the fields they want across nested relationships, avoiding REST's over-fetching (too many fields) and under-fetching (needing multiple calls). Trade-offs: caching and rate limiting are trickier than with REST.
explanations.C: Authentication is orthogonal to the query language — both REST and GraphQL need it.
explanations.D: Committing transactions is a server-side database concern; GraphQL mutations describe intent but do not bypass the server's transaction logic.

mcqs.question: Change Data Capture (CDC) is a pattern in which:

options.A: The full table is re-extracted on every run
options.B: Machine-learning models retrain on every new record
options.C: Data is encrypted at the source before leaving the network
options.D: Only inserts, updates and deletes on the source are captured (often from the database log) and propagated downstream

explanations.A: Full re-extraction is the opposite of CDC — it's inefficient for large tables and is precisely what CDC avoids.
explanations.B: Retraining strategy is an ML-ops concern, not what CDC defines.
explanations.C: Encryption at source is a separate security control.
explanations.D: Correct. CDC tools (Debezium, Oracle GoldenGate, AWS DMS, Fivetran) tail the database transaction log (WAL / binlog / redo log) and stream every insert / update / delete as an event. This keeps downstream systems (warehouses, lakes, search indexes) in near-real-time sync with source OLTP stores, without heavy re-extracts.

mcqs.question: An idempotent data pipeline is one where:

options.A: Running it multiple times with the same input produces the same result as running it once
options.B: It can only be run once per day
options.C: It requires manual approval between stages
options.D: It transforms data into a new format every run

explanations.A: Correct. An idempotent operation yields the same state no matter how many times it runs. For pipelines this typically means using merge / upsert (not append) semantics keyed on natural keys, deterministic partitioning, and replayable ingestion. Idempotency is essential for robustness — retries, backfills and failure recovery all depend on it.
explanations.B: Scheduling frequency doesn't define idempotency.
explanations.C: Manual gates are governance controls, not idempotency.
explanations.D: Changing format each run is actually the opposite of reproducible, deterministic behaviour.

mcqs.question: The tool 'dbt' (data build tool) is used primarily to:

options.A: Orchestrate container deployments in Kubernetes
options.B: Ingest Kafka topics into a lakehouse
options.C: Define, test and version-control SQL transformations that run inside a data warehouse (the 'T' in ELT)
options.D: Label training data for supervised machine learning

explanations.A: Kubernetes orchestration is done by Helm / Argo / Kustomize, not dbt.
explanations.B: Ingestion is the job of tools like Fivetran, Airbyte, Kafka Connect — not dbt.
explanations.C: Correct. dbt is a SQL-first transformation framework: engineers define models (SELECTs), tests, documentation and lineage in version-controlled files; dbt compiles and runs them in the warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres). It is the de-facto standard for the 'T' in modern ELT stacks.
explanations.D: Labelling is an ML-ops concern (Labelbox, Scale, Prodigy). dbt is about SQL transformations.

mcqs.question: In a data pipeline, 'backfilling' refers to:

options.A: Adding encryption to an existing stream
options.B: Re-running historical computations for a past date range, typically after a code fix or new feature
options.C: Forwarding every record to archive
options.D: Deleting incorrect records from production

explanations.A: Encryption is a security control, not a scheduling pattern.
explanations.B: Correct. Backfilling replays a pipeline across a historical date range — commonly used after fixing a bug, introducing a new column, or onboarding a new source. Frameworks like Airflow, Dagster and dbt provide first-class support (`--backfill`, catch-up runs). Idempotent pipelines make backfills safe because re-runs don't duplicate data.
explanations.C: Archiving is a lifecycle activity, not backfilling.
explanations.D: Deleting records is 'cleanup' or 'reversal' — separate from reconstructing history.

mcqs.question: A classical data warehouse typically employs:

options.A: Schema-on-read with no structural constraints
options.B: A graph database with SPARQL endpoints
options.C: A document-oriented NoSQL store
options.D: Schema-on-write with curated, structured tables optimised for BI

explanations.A: Schema-on-read is the lake pattern, where structure is applied at query time. Warehouses enforce schema when data is loaded.
explanations.B: Graph databases serve different workloads (network analysis, knowledge graphs). SPARQL is an RDF query language, not a warehouse pattern.
explanations.C: Document stores (MongoDB, Couchbase) are operational databases, not warehouses.
explanations.D: Correct. A warehouse enforces schema-on-write: data is cleansed, conformed and loaded into dimensional structures (star / snowflake schemas) before it becomes queryable. This produces consistent, high-quality, analytics-optimised data at the cost of upfront modelling and ETL.

mcqs.question: A data lake is characterised by:

options.A: Schema-on-read storage of raw data in diverse formats on low-cost object / distributed storage
options.B: Strictly normalised relational schemas
options.C: Only structured tabular data
options.D: Mandatory ACID transactions on every read

explanations.A: Correct. A data lake stores raw data in its native format (CSV, JSON, Parquet, Avro, images, logs) on cheap scalable storage (HDFS or object storage like S3, GCS, Azure ADLS). Schema is applied at query time ('schema-on-read'), which supports exploration and ML on diverse data but trades away the governance and ACID guarantees of a warehouse.
explanations.B: Normalised relational schemas are OLTP or warehouse territory, not lakes.
explanations.C: Lakes accept any format — including unstructured (audio, images, free text).
explanations.D: Plain lakes lack ACID. Adding ACID via an open table format (Delta / Iceberg / Hudi) is what turns a lake into a lakehouse.

mcqs.question: What do Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake and Apache Hudi have in common?

options.A: They are open table formats that add ACID, schema evolution and time-travel on top of files (typically Parquet) in object storage
options.B: They are proprietary storage engines bundled only with one cloud provider
options.C: They are OLTP database systems replacing PostgreSQL
options.D: They are classification taxonomies under the AI Act

explanations.A: Correct. Iceberg, Delta and Hudi are the three major open table formats of the lakehouse era. They maintain metadata (snapshots, manifests) alongside Parquet data files and add warehouse-grade features: ACID transactions, schema evolution, partition evolution, time-travel, efficient upserts and deletes. They make BI and ML work on the same object-store dataset.
explanations.B: All three are open-source and work on multiple clouds. Vendor lock-in is specifically what they push back against.
explanations.C: None of the three is an OLTP engine. They target analytics workloads at scale.
explanations.D: Nothing to do with AI Act tiers.

mcqs.question: Parquet, ORC and Arrow are all examples of:

options.A: Columnar storage / memory formats optimised for analytics workloads
options.B: Row-oriented OLTP table formats
options.C: Encryption schemes for personal data
options.D: Regulatory reporting XBRL taxonomies

explanations.A: Correct. Parquet (on-disk), ORC (on-disk) and Arrow (in-memory) are columnar formats: values of each column are stored together, enabling compression, vectorised execution and efficient column-pruning for analytical queries. This is why lakehouses, warehouses and distributed SQL engines rely on them.
explanations.B: Row-oriented layouts (typical of OLTP) store values of one record contiguously — inefficient for analytics that scan a few columns over many rows.
explanations.C: They are not encryption schemes — encryption is a separate layer that can be applied on top of any of these formats.
explanations.D: XBRL is a regulatory-reporting language; unrelated to these columnar formats.

mcqs.question: The 3 Vs of big data (classical model) stand for:

options.A: Volume, Variety, Velocity
options.B: Value, Validity, Veracity
options.C: Vector, Vertex, Vault
options.D: Virtual, Visual, Voluntary

explanations.A: Correct. Volume (how much), Variety (structured, semi-structured, unstructured formats) and Velocity (how fast it arrives) are the classical 3 Vs coined by Doug Laney. The model is often extended to 5 Vs with Veracity (trust / quality) and Value (extracted worth), and sometimes 7 Vs (adding Variability and Visualisation).
explanations.B: Those are often added as the 4th / 5th Vs but aren't the original three.
explanations.C: Unrelated jargon.
explanations.D: Unrelated — not the definition.

mcqs.question: 'Polyglot persistence' as a platform principle means:

options.A: Every application must expose its data through a single REST endpoint
options.B: Data must always be stored in plaintext so any language can read it
options.C: All data is stored in multiple languages for translation purposes
options.D: Different parts of a system use different data stores tailored to their workload (relational, document, key-value, graph, time-series, search…)

explanations.A: A single endpoint is an API-layer pattern, not a persistence-layer one.
explanations.B: Plaintext storage contradicts sound security practice and is unrelated to polyglot persistence.
explanations.C: Despite the 'linguistic' name, polyglot persistence has nothing to do with human languages.
explanations.D: Correct. Polyglot persistence (Fowler, 2011) argues that a modern system is rarely best served by a single data store; instead, each capability uses the store best suited to its access pattern — relational for complex transactions, key-value (Redis) for caches, document (MongoDB) for flexible entities, search (Elasticsearch) for full-text, graph (Neo4j) for relationships, time-series (InfluxDB) for metrics.

mcqs.question: A 'vector database' (Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus) is optimised for:

options.A: Storing financial ledger transactions with strict ACID guarantees
options.B: Running OLTP workloads at millisecond commit latency
options.C: Serving static HTML pages with edge caching
options.D: Performing approximate nearest-neighbour searches over high-dimensional embeddings

explanations.A: Financial ledgers use traditional ACID OLTP databases.
explanations.B: OLTP is served by row-oriented RDBMSs, not vector stores.
explanations.C: Static-page serving is a CDN / edge-cache concern.
explanations.D: Correct. Vector databases index high-dimensional embeddings (from LLMs or other ML models) and accept similarity queries — approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search using algorithms like HNSW or IVF. They power semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and recommendation systems that need similarity rather than exact matches.

mcqs.question: An 'OLAP cube' is best described as:

options.A: A physical server form factor
options.B: A container image format
options.C: A multi-dimensional data structure pre-aggregated along business dimensions (time, geography, product…) for fast slice-and-dice analysis
options.D: A hardware encryption appliance

explanations.A: Cube here is a logical / mathematical metaphor, not a server chassis.
explanations.B: Container images are packaged by OCI formats (Docker, OCI), unrelated.
explanations.C: Correct. OLAP cubes (SSAS, Essbase, Mondrian) pre-aggregate measures (sales, counts) along dimensions so analysts can slice, dice, drill-down and roll-up interactively. Modern in-memory engines (Power BI / Tabular, ClickHouse, DuckDB, Druid) increasingly compute on the fly, blurring the classic cube concept but keeping the multi-dimensional mental model.
explanations.D: Encryption appliances are HSMs / TPMs, unrelated.

mcqs.question: Modern cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift RA3) separate compute from storage primarily to:

options.A: Reduce query latency to microseconds on small workloads
options.B: Eliminate the need for SQL as a query language
options.C: Guarantee strict ACID across transactional and analytical systems alike
options.D: Scale compute and storage independently, enabling elasticity and isolating workloads

explanations.A: Separation sometimes adds latency compared with shared-nothing local disks; the benefit is elsewhere.
explanations.B: SQL remains the primary interface for all three systems.
explanations.C: ACID is not the point — modern warehouses add it to analytics storage, but OLTP systems remain separate.
explanations.D: Correct. Separating storage (object store, once-written, shared) from compute (ephemeral clusters / warehouses / slots) means you can scale each dimension independently. Different teams can get their own compute cluster on the same data without contention, and storage costs stay cheap. Snowflake's virtual warehouses, BigQuery slots and Redshift managed storage all embody this design.

mcqs.question: 'Descriptive analytics' primarily answers the question:

options.A: What should we do next?
options.B: What will happen?
options.C: What happened?
options.D: Why did it happen?

explanations.A: Prescriptive analytics answers 'what should we do?' — optimisation, decision support.
explanations.B: Predictive analytics answers 'what will happen?' — forecasting, classification.
explanations.C: Correct. Descriptive analytics answers 'what happened?' — dashboards, standard reports, KPIs summarising past performance. It's the bottom rung of the analytics-maturity ladder: descriptive → diagnostic → predictive → prescriptive.
explanations.D: Diagnostic analytics answers 'why did it happen?' — drill-downs, root-cause analysis.

mcqs.question: Self-service BI is characterised by:

options.A: Business users building their own reports on governed datasets without an IT ticket
options.B: IT exclusively preparing all analyses
options.C: Purely manual paper-based reporting
options.D: Deploying Kafka clusters by the finance team

explanations.A: Correct. Self-service BI (Power BI, Tableau, Qlik, Metabase, Looker) empowers business users to build reports, slice/dice and share insights without waiting for an IT backlog. To stay safe, it needs governed semantic layers (certified datasets, shared measures) and good glossaries — otherwise multiple versions of the truth proliferate.
explanations.B: Central IT prepping everything is the old waterfall model — the opposite of self-service.
explanations.C: Paper reporting pre-dates BI altogether.
explanations.D: Operating data infrastructure is a data-engineering task, not a BI user's role.

mcqs.question: A BI 'semantic layer' (e.g. Power BI datasets, Looker's LookML, Cube, dbt Semantic Layer) primarily provides:

options.A: A shared, governed definition of metrics and dimensions that multiple tools and users can query consistently
options.B: An encryption layer for in-transit data
options.C: A visualisation theme for corporate dashboards
options.D: A vector index for semantic search

explanations.A: Correct. A semantic layer sits between raw data and consumers: it defines metrics ('net revenue'), dimensions ('region') and their relationships once, so every dashboard, ad-hoc query and embedded analytics uses the same formula. This solves the 'same term, different number' problem that plagues ungoverned self-service BI.
explanations.B: TLS / encryption belongs to the transport / security layer.
explanations.C: Themes are visual styling, not semantic definitions.
explanations.D: 'Semantic' overlaps with the ML world ('semantic search') but in BI it refers to the metrics-definition layer.

mcqs.question: The 'SMART' acronym applied to KPIs stands for:

options.A: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
options.B: Strategic, Measurable, Automated, Reported, Timely
options.C: Simple, Managed, Aggregated, Reviewed, Transparent
options.D: Scalable, Modular, Abstract, Reliable, Tractable

explanations.A: Correct. SMART is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound — the classical goal-setting checklist, widely applied to KPI design. A KPI that is vague, not measurable, unattainable, irrelevant or lacks a time dimension will fail to drive behaviour.
explanations.B: Contains 'automated' and 'strategic' — not the SMART letters.
explanations.C: Reasonable-sounding but not SMART.
explanations.D: Those are software-engineering qualities — unrelated.

mcqs.question: A transport agency uses a model to recommend the optimal route given traffic, weather and accident probability. This is an example of:

options.A: Descriptive analytics
options.B: Diagnostic analytics
options.C: Predictive analytics
options.D: Prescriptive analytics

explanations.A: Descriptive would report past travel times — it doesn't recommend a course of action.
explanations.B: Diagnostic would explain why a particular route was slow yesterday — a root-cause analysis, not a recommendation.
explanations.C: Predictive would forecast the travel time or arrival probability. Recommending the optimal action is a step beyond prediction.
explanations.D: Correct. Prescriptive analytics uses optimisation, simulation or decision-support techniques on top of predictive outputs to recommend the best action under constraints. Route-optimisation systems typically combine predictive models (traffic, weather) with an optimisation layer (shortest-time path under constraints).

mcqs.question: Compared to a 'report', a 'dashboard' in BI typically:

options.A: Offers at-a-glance, visual summaries of KPIs with interactivity
options.B: Provides a rich narrative with many pages of text
options.C: Is delivered exclusively on paper
options.D: Contains only raw tables with no visuals

explanations.A: Correct. Dashboards combine KPIs, charts and filters into a visual summary optimised for quick comprehension. They often support interactivity — drill-down, slicing — and refresh frequently. Reports tend to be longer, narrative and delivered on a schedule.
explanations.B: Narrative reports (annual reports, audit reports) rely on prose; dashboards rely on visuals.
explanations.C: Paper delivery is rare in modern BI; dashboards are typically interactive web artefacts.
explanations.D: Raw tables are the opposite of a dashboard's visual summary.

mcqs.question: Monthly customer-satisfaction survey score is best described as a:

options.A: Leading indicator that anticipates future revenue trends
options.B: Lagging indicator of past customer experience
options.C: Operational indicator with no strategic value
options.D: Raw metric incompatible with KPI classification

explanations.A: Correct. Customer-satisfaction scores measure current perception, which tends to precede (lead) retention and revenue outcomes — people who are unhappy today churn tomorrow. Leading indicators are action-oriented because you can still influence the downstream outcome. They are contrasted with lagging indicators (quarterly revenue, annual churn) which confirm what has already happened.
explanations.B: Lagging indicators reflect results that have already crystallised — revenue, churn, completed projects.
explanations.C: Customer satisfaction is a classical strategic KPI, not merely operational.
explanations.D: Any quantifiable measure can be a metric and, if tied to an objective, a KPI.

mcqs.question: You want to visualise how a single KPI varies across a hierarchy of categories (e.g. country → region → city) and show relative size. The most appropriate chart is:

options.A: A pie chart with 30 slices
options.B: A stacked line chart over time
options.C: A treemap
options.D: A 3D rotating bar chart

explanations.A: Pie charts fail beyond a handful of categories. Thirty slices are impossible to compare visually.
explanations.B: Stacked line charts are for time series with components. They don't handle a nested geographic hierarchy well.
explanations.C: Correct. Treemaps display hierarchical data using nested rectangles sized by a metric — excellent for 'which countries / regions / cities contribute most' at a glance. They were designed precisely to visualise hierarchies with relative magnitudes on limited screen space.
explanations.D: 3D bar charts distort perception and are widely discouraged (Tufte, Few). They are almost never the right choice.

mcqs.question: A 'reporting cadence' defines:

options.A: The speed of the network between source and dashboard
options.B: The encryption algorithm used for reports in transit
options.C: The agreed frequency at which a report or KPI is refreshed and reviewed
options.D: The visualisation library used on the front-end

explanations.A: Network speed is a technical performance concern, not cadence.
explanations.B: Encryption algorithms (TLS, AES) are orthogonal to reporting cadence.
explanations.C: Correct. Reporting cadence is the agreed frequency — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly — at which a report or KPI is refreshed and reviewed. Cadence must match the decision it supports: daily ops dashboards for production, monthly for financial close, quarterly for strategic reviews. Over-cadencing wastes effort; under-cadencing misses signals.
explanations.D: Visualisation library choice (ECharts, D3, Plotly, Vega-Lite) is a technical/UX decision unrelated to cadence.

mcqs.question: Which pair best distinguishes master data from reference data?

options.A: Master data = ISO country codes; reference data = customer records
options.B: Master data = training datasets; reference data = test datasets
options.C: Master data = transactional events; reference data = historical archives
options.D: Master data = key business entities (customer, product, employee); reference data = controlled code lists (country, currency, status)

explanations.A: Reversed. Country codes are reference data; customer records are master data.
explanations.B: ML training / test sets are unrelated to the master / reference distinction.
explanations.C: Transactions are transactional data, not master. Master data describes the entities transactions refer to.
explanations.D: Correct. Master data = the nouns the business operates on (customer, product, employee, supplier, location). Reference data = closed, relatively static code lists used for classification (country ISO-3166 codes, currency ISO-4217 codes, ISIN, status enums). Both feed into other systems; master data is usually richer and changes with the business.

mcqs.question: In MDM, a 'golden record' is:

options.A: The most recent edit of any record in any source system
options.B: The training dataset used to retrain a fraud-detection model
options.C: A copy of the production database kept for disaster recovery
options.D: A single, reconciled, authoritative view of a master entity merged from multiple source systems

explanations.A: 'Most recent edit' can still be wrong, incomplete or contradict another system — that's not what 'golden' means.
explanations.B: Training datasets are ML artefacts, not MDM records.
explanations.C: DR copies are operational backups, unrelated.
explanations.D: Correct. A golden record is the canonical, de-duplicated, reconciled view of a master entity (customer, product). MDM tools apply matching rules (deterministic and probabilistic), survivorship rules (which source wins for which attribute) and stewardship workflows to produce it. Downstream systems rely on the golden record as the source of truth.

mcqs.question: In MDM matching and merging, a 'survivorship rule' determines:

options.A: Which records are archived to cold storage
options.B: Which attribute value wins when several source systems disagree about the same entity
options.C: Which employees survive organisational restructuring
options.D: Which node in a Kafka cluster handles the next message

explanations.A: Archiving is a lifecycle task, unrelated to survivorship.
explanations.B: Correct. When two or more source systems hold conflicting values for the same attribute of the same entity (e.g. customer address in CRM vs ERP), survivorship rules decide which one prevails in the golden record — most recent update, most trusted source, highest quality score, or manual steward decision.
explanations.C: HR humour aside, it's not what MDM means.
explanations.D: Kafka partition assignment is a different technical topic.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT typically considered reference data?

options.A: ISO 3166 country codes
options.B: ISO 4217 currency codes
options.C: Individual customer transactions
options.D: Human-Resources job-grade categories

explanations.A: Country codes are canonical reference data — stable, controlled, widely referenced.
explanations.B: Currency codes are textbook reference data.
explanations.C: Correct. Individual customer transactions are transactional data, not reference. They are high-volume, event-like, and reference back to master entities (customer, product). Reference data, by contrast, is small, stable and shared across many systems.
explanations.D: HR job grades are a controlled code list — a reference-data use case.

mcqs.question: IATE (Inter-Active Terminology for Europe) supports EU multilingualism by providing:

options.A: A controlled, inter-institutional terminology database of concepts in 24 languages
options.B: Machine-translation training corpora
options.C: A legal register of EU treaties
options.D: The public-procurement vocabulary (CPV)

explanations.A: Correct. IATE is the EU's inter-institutional terminology database, maintained collaboratively by translators and terminologists across Commission, Parliament, Council, Court of Justice, Committee of the Regions, ECB and other bodies. Each concept card carries definitions, context, sources and equivalents across the 24 official languages.
explanations.B: MT corpora are separate linguistic resources (e.g. ELRC, OPUS, EuroParl). IATE is a terminology database, not a parallel corpus.
explanations.C: The treaties register is separate (EUR-Lex). IATE is about terminology, not full legal texts.
explanations.D: CPV (Common Procurement Vocabulary) is a classification for tenders, hosted on TED — distinct from IATE.

mcqs.question: EuroVoc is used by EU institutions to:

options.A: Serve as the EU's translation memory
options.B: Index documents across institutions using a multilingual thesaurus
options.C: Authenticate users on the Interoperable Europe Portal
options.D: Provide SQL access to the Official Journal archive

explanations.A: The EU translation memory is Euramis.
explanations.B: Correct. EuroVoc is the EU's multilingual, multidisciplinary thesaurus, structured according to SKOS, used to classify and index documents across EU institutions. It covers all EU policy fields and is available in 24 languages plus partner languages. Publications Office uses it to tag EUR-Lex content.
explanations.C: Authentication is handled by EU Login / eIDAS, not EuroVoc.
explanations.D: EuroVoc is a vocabulary, not a database engine or query interface.

mcqs.question: A 'registry-style' MDM architecture differs from a 'repository-style' MDM primarily because:

options.A: Registry stores only an index pointing to source systems; repository stores the golden record itself
options.B: Registry is an open-data concept; repository is a closed-data concept
options.C: Registry applies to customer data; repository to product data
options.D: Registry is legally required by the DGA; repository is optional

explanations.A: Correct. In a registry-style MDM, the MDM hub holds only keys and cross-references (who holds what data about which entity) — source systems remain the system of record. In a repository (centralised) style, the MDM hub stores the golden record itself and systems consume from it. Registry is lighter but leaves survivorship distributed; repository is more authoritative but heavier to build.
explanations.B: Not a meaningful distinction — neither style is tied to open vs closed data.
explanations.C: MDM styles apply to any master domain.
explanations.D: The DGA doesn't mandate either architecture.

mcqs.question: The EU 'Core Business Vocabulary' (SEMIC) provides:

options.A: A mandatory ontology for banks under MiFID II
options.B: A catalogue of consulting firms recognised by the Commission
options.C: A controlled list of business-sector NACE codes
options.D: A minimum reusable data model to describe legal entities across public services

explanations.A: MiFID II has its own regulatory-reporting standards unrelated to Core Business.
explanations.B: No such catalogue is linked to Core Business.
explanations.C: NACE codes are a separate Eurostat classification.
explanations.D: Correct. The Core Business Vocabulary is one of the SEMIC Core Vocabularies (alongside Core Person, Core Location, Core Public Service). It offers a minimum, reusable, extensible model to describe a legal entity — its name, identifiers, legal status, addresses — so cross-border public services interoperate semantically.

mcqs.question: The Legal Entity Identifier (LEI, ISO 17442) is:

options.A: A 10-character alphanumeric code issued under eIDAS
options.B: A 20-character alphanumeric code uniquely identifying legal entities engaging in financial transactions worldwide
options.C: A GDPR pseudonym for natural persons
options.D: A registration scheme under the Open Data Directive

explanations.A: eIDAS concerns electronic identification and trust services, not LEI.
explanations.B: Correct. The LEI is a 20-character alphanumeric identifier (ISO 17442) assigned through the Global Legal Entity Identifier System (GLEIS), administered by GLEIF. It uniquely identifies legal entities participating in financial transactions and is widely mandated in financial regulation (EMIR, MiFID II, SFTR).
explanations.C: LEIs identify legal entities, not natural persons.
explanations.D: The Open Data Directive doesn't define the LEI.

mcqs.question: Metadata can be defined as:

options.A: Data about data — describing its content, context, structure and lineage
options.B: Data that has been encrypted
options.C: Data older than seven years
options.D: Data exclusively about machine-learning models

explanations.A: Correct. Metadata is descriptive, structural, administrative or provenance information about a data asset — title, author, creation date, schema, lineage, access rules, quality indicators. Good metadata makes data findable, understandable and trustworthy, which is why it anchors FAIR and modern data-catalogue practice.
explanations.B: Encryption is a security operation, not a definition of metadata.
explanations.C: Age isn't part of the definition of metadata.
explanations.D: Metadata exists for any data — datasets, documents, images, ML models (model cards are a subset).

mcqs.question: Dublin Core provides a set of:

options.A: 15 core metadata elements for describing resources (title, creator, date, subject, …)
options.B: Cybersecurity controls for EU institutions
options.C: EU public-procurement categories
options.D: ML model evaluation metrics

explanations.A: Correct. Dublin Core (DCMI) specifies 15 generic metadata elements — title, creator, subject, description, publisher, contributor, date, type, format, identifier, source, language, relation, coverage, rights — widely used for cataloguing resources. It is a foundational schema referenced by DCAT and other metadata vocabularies.
explanations.B: Cybersecurity controls come from ISO 27001, NIS2, etc. — not Dublin Core.
explanations.C: Procurement codes live in CPV, not Dublin Core.
explanations.D: ML metrics (accuracy, precision, recall) are unrelated to metadata schemas.

mcqs.question: Data lineage in a catalogue tracks:

options.A: The origin of data and the transformations it undergoes from source to consumption
options.B: The geographic origin of data-centre hardware
options.C: Only the login history of analysts
options.D: The training runs of ML models

explanations.A: Correct. Data lineage answers 'where did this number come from?' by tracing data through sources, pipelines, transformations and consuming reports. It is essential for impact analysis (what breaks if this table changes?), regulatory accountability and root-cause analysis. Modern catalogues auto-ingest lineage from dbt, Airflow, Spark and warehouse query logs.
explanations.B: Hardware origin is a supply-chain / sovereignty concern, not data lineage.
explanations.C: Login history is an audit log, not lineage.
explanations.D: ML-model provenance is related but a different layer (model lineage) from data lineage.

mcqs.question: The EU open-data portal data.europa.eu federates datasets from:

options.A: Only Commission departments
options.B: Member-State public-sector data portals, EU institutions, bodies and agencies, based on DCAT-AP
options.C: Private-sector e-commerce platforms
options.D: OECD and UN data repositories exclusively

explanations.A: The portal aggregates far more than Commission DGs.
explanations.B: Correct. data.europa.eu harvests metadata from Member-State open-data portals (typically via DCAT-AP), EU institutions, bodies and agencies, and publishes a single searchable catalogue. It is the successor to the former European Data Portal and EU Open Data Portal, consolidated in 2021.
explanations.C: Private e-commerce is out of scope.
explanations.D: Non-EU international datasets are not the core of the portal, though interoperability with some is possible through shared vocabularies.

mcqs.question: On the data.europa.eu ecosystem, a dataset containing meteorological observations would likely be described using:

options.A: GeoDCAT-AP
options.B: StatDCAT-AP
options.C: The HVD application profile of DCAT-AP
options.D: A and C are both commonly applicable

explanations.A: GeoDCAT-AP is the geospatial profile. Meteorological data is often geo-referenced (station locations, grids), so GeoDCAT-AP often applies.
explanations.B: StatDCAT-AP targets statistical datasets (SDMX-style aggregates). Raw observations are not typically statistical aggregates.
explanations.C: The HVD profile applies because 'meteorological' is one of the six High-Value-Dataset categories under Implementing Reg. 2023/138.
explanations.D: Correct. A real meteorological dataset would typically be described with the HVD profile (because it falls in an HVD category) AND GeoDCAT-AP (because it is geospatial). Profiles are composable; using both maximises interoperability across the portal ecosystem.

mcqs.question: Which is the most accurate distinction between a taxonomy and an ontology?

options.A: Taxonomies hierarchically classify concepts; ontologies formally specify concepts, relationships and axioms with a logic reasoners can use
options.B: Taxonomies are always richer than ontologies
options.C: They are synonyms
options.D: Ontologies always use JSON while taxonomies use XML

explanations.A: Correct. A taxonomy is a hierarchy — broader/narrower relations, classifying items into classes (think biological Linnaean classification, or NACE). An ontology adds formal relationships (partOf, hasRole, inverseOf), classes, properties, cardinalities and logical axioms that an automated reasoner (Pellet, HermiT) can use to infer new facts. OWL is the canonical ontology language.
explanations.B: The opposite — ontologies are richer because they capture more semantics.
explanations.C: They are related but distinct. Using them interchangeably loses important precision.
explanations.D: Serialisation (RDF/XML, Turtle, JSON-LD) is orthogonal to the taxonomy-vs-ontology distinction.

mcqs.question: A 'data catalogue' in a modern data platform (e.g. Collibra, OpenMetadata, DataHub) typically provides:

options.A: Only physical table definitions
options.B: A container orchestration platform
options.C: An encryption key rotation service
options.D: Searchable inventory of data assets with metadata, ownership, lineage, quality and business glossary integration

explanations.A: Physical definitions are only part of what a catalogue provides.
explanations.B: Container orchestration is Kubernetes's job.
explanations.C: Key rotation belongs to a KMS / HSM, not a catalogue.
explanations.D: Correct. Modern data catalogues are the 'Google for your data' — users search for datasets, see definitions, owners, certified vs experimental status, lineage, quality scores and related glossary terms. They are anchor points for self-service analytics and for governance programmes.

mcqs.question: 'Semantic search' over documents, as increasingly implemented in EU libraries and portals, relies primarily on:

options.A: Exact keyword matching with Boolean operators
options.B: Dense vector embeddings capturing semantic similarity, often combined with traditional keyword search (hybrid)
options.C: SQL full-text LIKE queries
options.D: Regex substitutions in the documents

explanations.A: Keyword / Boolean search is lexical, not semantic — it misses synonyms, paraphrases and cross-language matches.
explanations.B: Correct. Semantic search encodes documents and queries as dense vector embeddings (from language models), and retrieves by similarity (cosine, inner product). In production it is typically hybrid: lexical (BM25) + semantic, often with a re-ranker. This gives recall on paraphrases while keeping exact-match precision.
explanations.C: LIKE is lexical and pattern-based — not semantic.
explanations.D: Regex is pattern matching, not meaning-based retrieval.

mcqs.question: DOIs, ORCIDs and Handle.net identifiers are examples of:

options.A: Encryption key types
options.B: European public-procurement codes
options.C: Data-warehouse storage formats
options.D: Persistent identifiers that make resources findable over time, supporting the 'F' in FAIR

explanations.A: Keys are secrets, not identifiers.
explanations.B: Procurement codes live in CPV.
explanations.C: Storage formats are Parquet, ORC, Avro, etc.
explanations.D: Correct. DOI (Digital Object Identifier), ORCID (researcher IDs) and Handle.net are persistent identifier systems. They guarantee that a reference resolves to the same (or updated) resource over time, surviving URL changes and institutional reorganisations. Persistent IDs directly support the 'Findable' principle of FAIR (F1: unique persistent identifier).

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an 'inherent' data-quality characteristic under ISO/IEC 25012?

options.A: Availability
options.B: Accuracy
options.C: Portability
options.D: Recoverability

explanations.A: Availability is system-dependent — it depends on infrastructure uptime, not the data itself.
explanations.B: Correct. ISO/IEC 25012 splits 15 characteristics into inherent (accuracy, completeness, consistency, credibility, currentness) and system-dependent (the other ten). Accuracy — the degree to which data correctly represents the real-world value — is an inherent property of the data regardless of the system storing it.
explanations.C: Portability depends on the surrounding system (formats, migration tools).
explanations.D: Recoverability depends on backup and restore mechanisms — system-dependent.

mcqs.question: 'Data profiling' involves:

options.A: Predicting a user's preferences from past behaviour
options.B: Encrypting personal data at rest
options.C: Analysing datasets to understand structure, content, distributions and quality issues
options.D: Producing GDPR privacy notices

explanations.A: User profiling is an ML / marketing concept, unrelated.
explanations.B: Encryption is a security control.
explanations.C: Correct. Data profiling is the systematic examination of a dataset — distributions, null rates, uniqueness, length patterns, value frequencies, cross-column dependencies — to understand its quality and structure. It is the essential first step before cleansing, modelling or integration. Tools include Great Expectations, Soda, pandas-profiling / ydata-profiling, Dataedo.
explanations.D: Privacy notices are a transparency obligation under GDPR Arts. 13–14.

mcqs.question: In data-quality terms, 'accuracy' and 'precision' differ because:

options.A: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is the level of granularity / repeatability of measurement
options.B: They are synonymous
options.C: Accuracy applies to text; precision to numbers
options.D: Accuracy applies only to ML models

explanations.A: Correct. Accuracy answers 'is the value right?' — closeness to the true real-world value. Precision answers 'how finely / consistently is it measured?' — number of digits, repeatability, granularity. A GPS can be precise (5 decimal places) but inaccurate (shifted by 10 m); and vice versa. Both are ISO/IEC 25012 quality characteristics.
explanations.B: They describe different things; conflating them is a common source of confusion.
explanations.C: Both apply to numeric and structured data in general.
explanations.D: Accuracy predates ML and applies to any data domain; in ML, 'accuracy' also names a specific classification metric (TP+TN)/N, but the DQ concept is broader.

mcqs.question: Monitoring 'completeness' on an incoming customer feed means checking:

options.A: Whether the data is encrypted end-to-end
options.B: Whether all required attributes are populated for each record and whether expected record counts are received
options.C: Whether records are logically consistent with each other
options.D: Whether the records are available to all departments

explanations.A: Encryption is a security property.
explanations.B: Correct. Completeness has two common dimensions: (1) record-level — all required attributes filled (no unexpected NULLs where values must exist), (2) dataset-level — expected volume received (no partial batches, no missing delta). It is one of the five inherent ISO/IEC 25012 characteristics and a frequent failure mode in pipelines.
explanations.C: Consistency is its own characteristic — agreement between related data.
explanations.D: Availability / accessibility is a system-dependent characteristic, not completeness.

mcqs.question: A data-quality rule stating 'total order amount must equal the sum of line-item amounts' targets which dimension?

options.A: Completeness
options.B: Consistency (internal consistency across related attributes)
options.C: Accessibility
options.D: Portability

explanations.A: Completeness would check whether the total and line items are populated at all, not whether they agree.
explanations.B: Correct. This is a cross-attribute / cross-table consistency check: values at different levels of the record must agree by a known business rule. Consistency (ISO/IEC 25012) covers both same-record consistency and cross-system consistency. Such 'reconciliation' rules are at the heart of financial, invoicing and ERP DQ pipelines.
explanations.C: Accessibility is about whether data can be obtained by authorised users.
explanations.D: Portability is about moving data between systems — unrelated to arithmetic consistency.

mcqs.question: 'Data cleansing' refers to:

options.A: Deleting a production database
options.B: Detecting and correcting errors, inconsistencies, duplicates and missing values in a dataset
options.C: Encrypting sensitive fields in a database
options.D: Compressing Parquet files on object storage

explanations.A: That is destruction, not cleansing. Cleansing preserves data while improving it.
explanations.B: Correct. Data cleansing (scrubbing) detects quality issues — incorrect values, duplicates, misspellings, missing values, inconsistent formats — and corrects them. It's typically preceded by profiling and feeds into MDM, analytics or ML pipelines. Techniques include standardisation, deduplication, validation against reference lists, and record linkage.
explanations.C: Encryption doesn't improve quality — it protects confidentiality.
explanations.D: Compression is a storage optimisation.

mcqs.question: Great Expectations, Soda and dbt tests are all examples of:

options.A: Cloud providers
options.B: Notebook environments
options.C: Stream-processing engines
options.D: Tools that express and execute data-quality tests / assertions on datasets within a pipeline

explanations.A: None are cloud providers.
explanations.B: Notebooks are Jupyter, Zeppelin, Hex, Deepnote.
explanations.C: Stream processors are Flink, Kafka Streams, Spark Structured Streaming.
explanations.D: Correct. These tools let engineers express quality expectations in code — row counts, null rates, uniqueness, referential integrity, value ranges, accepted values, cross-column invariants. They run inside pipelines and fail fast when data violates expectations, often alerting or quarantining records. They are the 'testing framework' equivalent for data.

mcqs.question: In data-quality theory, 'fitness for use' implies that quality is:

options.A: Absolute — there is a single universal quality standard
options.B: Defined entirely by the originating system's vendor
options.C: Relative to the intended purpose of the data — the same dataset can be fit for one use and unfit for another
options.D: Synonymous with high precision

explanations.A: Absolute quality doesn't make sense across contexts — a dataset sufficient for a trend estimate may be unfit for billing.
explanations.B: Vendors describe the product; fitness is judged by the user in their context.
explanations.C: Correct. 'Fitness for use' (Juran) is the core insight of modern DQ: quality is context-dependent. Customer addresses good enough for targeted marketing may be unfit for legal notifications. DQ programmes therefore define quality by use case, with KPIs mapped to business purposes — not by a universal bar.
explanations.D: Precision is one dimension; fitness is the integration of multiple dimensions against the purpose.

mcqs.question: Record linkage (also called entity resolution) is the process of:

options.A: Cryptographically signing each record
options.B: Linking database tables via foreign keys
options.C: Identifying and matching records that refer to the same real-world entity across one or several data sources
options.D: Compressing duplicate rows with run-length encoding

explanations.A: Signing is a security / integrity operation, unrelated.
explanations.B: Foreign keys link by design; record linkage tackles the harder case where there's no shared key.
explanations.C: Correct. Record linkage identifies pairs of records that, despite differences in spelling, format or data quality, refer to the same person, organisation, product or location. Deterministic rules (exact match on keys) are combined with probabilistic methods (Fellegi-Sunter) and, increasingly, ML classifiers. It is central to MDM, fraud detection and census/statistics.
explanations.D: RLE is a compression technique, not a DQ concept.

mcqs.question: A 'data-quality scorecard' summarises:

options.A: CPU usage of analytics clusters
options.B: A set of DQ KPIs (completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness …) scored and tracked over time
options.C: The outcome of GDPR fines in the last year
options.D: The number of Kafka messages per day

explanations.A: CPU usage is an infrastructure metric.
explanations.B: Correct. A DQ scorecard presents a curated set of quality KPIs by domain (or by critical dataset), with traffic-light thresholds and trends. It gives governance bodies an at-a-glance view of where quality is improving or degrading, and drives investment prioritisation. It's the BI-for-DQ.
explanations.C: Fines are enforcement metrics — separate from internal DQ tracking.
explanations.D: Kafka throughput is a platform metric.

mcqs.question: What is the primary difference between RBAC and ABAC access-control models?

options.A: RBAC assigns permissions via roles; ABAC evaluates attributes of subject, resource, action and context in policy rules
options.B: RBAC is deprecated; ABAC is mandatory under GDPR
options.C: RBAC applies to SQL; ABAC applies to NoSQL only
options.D: RBAC requires biometric login; ABAC does not

explanations.A: Correct. RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) groups permissions into roles that users inherit. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) evaluates policies on attributes of the subject (department, clearance), the resource (classification, owner), the action (read, write) and the context (time, location, risk score). ABAC is more flexible and expressive; RBAC is simpler and easier to audit. Modern systems often combine both (RBAC for baseline, ABAC for fine-grained rules).
explanations.B: GDPR mandates appropriate technical measures but does not prescribe RBAC vs ABAC. Both are valid.
explanations.C: Both models are technology-neutral.
explanations.D: Biometrics are an authentication method, orthogonal to access-control models.

mcqs.ref: Encryption at rest vs in transit
mcqs.question: Encryption 'in transit' protects data while it is:

options.A: Stored on disk in a database
options.B: Moving over a network between systems
options.C: Being displayed on a screen
options.D: Being physically transported by courier

explanations.A: Storage-level encryption is 'at rest' — applied while the data sits on disk or in backup.
explanations.B: Correct. Encryption in transit protects data moving between systems, typically via TLS (HTTPS) or IPsec. Both at-rest and in-transit encryption are recommended baseline controls for personal data under GDPR Art. 32 and are almost always required for sensitive public-sector systems.
explanations.C: Display-layer protection is more about screen masking / UI controls than encryption.
explanations.D: Physical courier protection is 'physical security', not cryptographic in-transit encryption.

mcqs.question: Under GDPR Art. 35, a DPIA must be carried out when processing is likely to result in:

options.A: A high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
options.B: A marketing gain for the controller
options.C: Any level of technical complexity
options.D: A financial loss for the processor

explanations.A: Correct. Art. 35(1) mandates a DPIA whenever processing, especially using new technologies, is 'likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons'. Art. 35(3) adds specific triggers: systematic and extensive profiling producing legal effects, large-scale special-category processing, and systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas. National DPAs publish 'blacklists' and 'whitelists' to help controllers decide.
explanations.B: Commercial gain is irrelevant to the DPIA threshold.
explanations.C: Complexity alone does not trigger a DPIA.
explanations.D: Loss to the processor is a commercial risk, not a DPIA trigger.

mcqs.question: Art. 32 GDPR on security of processing requires measures 'appropriate to the risk', providing as examples:

options.A: Only passwords longer than 12 characters
options.B: Using blockchain for every record
options.C: Publishing all system logs openly
options.D: Pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience; ability to restore in a timely manner; regular testing

explanations.A: Art. 32 doesn't prescribe specific password lengths.
explanations.B: Blockchain is neither required nor sufficient — Art. 32 is technology-neutral.
explanations.C: Publishing logs openly would typically violate confidentiality.
explanations.D: Correct. Art. 32(1) lists examples of appropriate measures: pseudonymisation and encryption; ensuring ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience; ability to restore availability and access in a timely manner after an incident; and a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of the measures. Note the CIA triad sitting underneath.

mcqs.question: Art. 4(5) GDPR defines pseudonymisation as processing such that personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject:

options.A: Under any circumstances whatsoever
options.B: Without the use of additional information, which is kept separately and subject to technical and organisational measures
options.C: Only by the data subject themselves
options.D: Only by the national data-protection authority

explanations.A: If re-identification were impossible under all circumstances, it would be anonymisation, not pseudonymisation.
explanations.B: Correct. Pseudonymisation is explicitly reversible — with the separately held 'additional information' (e.g. a mapping table / key), the subject can be identified again. That's precisely why pseudonymised data stays personal data and remains in GDPR scope. The value of pseudonymisation is as a safeguard under Arts. 25 and 32, reducing risk of linkability.
explanations.C: The capability is held by whoever controls the additional information (typically the controller), not by the data subject alone.
explanations.D: DPAs have no special reversal capability — they investigate compliance.

mcqs.question: The 'principle of least privilege' in access control means:

options.A: Users and systems are granted only the permissions strictly required to perform their tasks
options.B: All users have administrator rights by default
options.C: Access is always granted for 24 hours and then revoked
options.D: Permissions are granted in alphabetical order

explanations.A: Correct. Least privilege (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975) requires that every user, service account or process operates with the minimum permissions needed for its function. This reduces blast radius on compromise and limits accidental damage. It complements separation of duties and underpins modern zero-trust and JIT-access patterns.
explanations.B: Blanket admin rights is the opposite of least privilege — and a security antipattern.
explanations.C: Time-bounding access (just-in-time) is related but not the definition of least privilege.
explanations.D: Alphabetical ordering has no security relevance.

mcqs.question: 'Differential privacy' is a privacy-protection technique that:

options.A: Encrypts all data end-to-end with quantum-resistant algorithms
options.B: Adds calibrated statistical noise to query outputs so that the presence or absence of any single individual does not measurably affect the result
options.C: Removes direct identifiers and replaces them with hashes
options.D: Is equivalent to pseudonymisation under GDPR

explanations.A: Differential privacy is not an encryption scheme — it is a statistical property of outputs.
explanations.B: Correct. Differential privacy (Dwork et al., 2006) is a mathematical framework guaranteeing that the inclusion or exclusion of any single individual in a dataset changes the probability of any query outcome by at most a bounded factor (ε). In practice, DP mechanisms add carefully calibrated noise (Laplace, Gaussian) to query outputs or to statistics. It is used by the US Census 2020, Apple and Google for telemetry, and is increasingly discussed for EU data-space secondary use.
explanations.C: Direct-identifier hashing is pseudonymisation, not DP.
explanations.D: DP is a stronger, quantitative privacy guarantee — not equivalent to the legal concept of pseudonymisation.

mcqs.question: The 'zero-trust' security model is best summarised by the slogan:

options.A: 'Trust but verify'
options.B: 'Never trust, always verify' — no implicit trust based on network location
options.C: 'Deny by default, allow by exception' only for databases
options.D: 'Always trust internal traffic'

explanations.A: 'Trust but verify' is the older perimeter-based philosophy. Zero-trust drops the initial trust part.
explanations.B: Correct. Zero trust abandons the perimeter assumption that 'inside the corporate network = safe'. Every request — internal or external — is authenticated, authorised and continuously verified against identity, device posture, and context. The NIST SP 800-207 model codifies the approach; mTLS, short-lived credentials and per-request policy decisions are hallmarks.
explanations.C: Deny-by-default is a firewall principle, not the full zero-trust story.
explanations.D: Trusting internal traffic is precisely what zero trust rejects.

mcqs.question: ISO/IEC 27001 is primarily:

options.A: A standard specifying requirements for an Information Security Management System (ISMS)
options.B: A standard for data-quality characteristics
options.C: An EU Directive on personal-data protection
options.D: A vocabulary for geospatial data

explanations.A: Correct. ISO/IEC 27001 sets requirements for an ISMS — an organisation-wide, risk-based management system for information security. It is the basis for certification audits. ISO/IEC 27002 (accompanying) provides a catalogue of security controls. Together they structure most public-sector and enterprise security programmes.
explanations.B: Data-quality characteristics are ISO/IEC 25012.
explanations.C: GDPR is the personal-data instrument, not ISO 27001.
explanations.D: Geospatial vocabularies include ISO 19115 (metadata) and others — not 27001.

mcqs.question: Clustering customers into segments without predefined labels is an example of:

options.A: Unsupervised learning
options.B: Supervised learning
options.C: Reinforcement learning
options.D: Federated learning

explanations.A: Correct. Clustering (k-means, DBSCAN, hierarchical, Gaussian mixtures) is the canonical unsupervised task: discover structure in data without given labels. Results need interpretation — the algorithm tells you 'here are groups' but not 'this group = high-value customers'. Anomaly detection and association rules are other unsupervised families.
explanations.B: Supervised learning requires labelled training examples (input → target).
explanations.C: Reinforcement learning is sequential decision-making with rewards (games, robotics, RLHF).
explanations.D: Federated learning is a distributed training method, not a task type.

mcqs.question: In ML best practice, the 'test set' is primarily used for:

options.A: Tuning hyperparameters during model development
options.B: Fitting the model parameters
options.C: Estimating the model's generalisation performance on unseen data, once only, after training and validation
options.D: Computing feature importances

explanations.A: Hyperparameter tuning is done on the validation set. Using the test set for tuning leaks information and inflates apparent performance.
explanations.B: Model parameters are fit on the training set.
explanations.C: Correct. The test set is held out, untouched during training and tuning, and used once at the end to report an unbiased estimate of generalisation performance. Repeated re-use of the test set effectively turns it into a validation set, biasing the reported metric.
explanations.D: Feature importances are computed on training data (or validation), not on the test set.

mcqs.question: A fraud-detection model with high recall but low precision will:

options.A: Miss many fraud cases while producing very few false alarms
options.B: Have high accuracy on non-fraud but low on fraud
options.C: Catch most fraud cases but raise many false alarms
options.D: Produce no fraud alerts at all

explanations.A: That would be low recall + high precision — the opposite.
explanations.B: That's an accuracy-on-subclasses claim, not the precision-recall trade-off.
explanations.C: Correct. Recall = TP / (TP + FN) — the share of actual frauds captured. Precision = TP / (TP + FP) — the share of alerts that are true frauds. High recall / low precision means the model catches most fraud (few false negatives) but with many false alarms (many false positives). In fraud detection, this is often acceptable because missing fraud is costlier than investigating a false alert.
explanations.D: No alerts at all would mean zero recall.

mcqs.question: A model that achieves near-perfect training accuracy but significantly lower accuracy on a held-out test set is most likely:

options.A: Underfitting
options.B: Perfectly calibrated
options.C: Overfitting — memorising training data, failing to generalise
options.D: Suffering from label noise only on the test set

explanations.A: Underfitting shows poor performance on both training and test — the model is too simple.
explanations.B: Calibration is about predicted probabilities matching observed frequencies — different concept.
explanations.C: Correct. Overfitting happens when a model captures idiosyncratic noise in the training data instead of underlying signal. Symptoms: training loss near zero while validation / test loss is high. Remedies include regularisation (L1/L2, dropout), simpler models, more data, data augmentation, early stopping and cross-validation.
explanations.D: Label noise on the test set would lower apparent performance across the board, not just the gap between train and test.

mcqs.question: Predicting house prices in euros from features like size, location and age is an example of a:

options.A: Classification task
options.B: Clustering task
options.C: Regression task
options.D: Reinforcement-learning task

explanations.A: Classification predicts categorical labels (spam / not spam, fraud / not fraud).
explanations.B: Clustering groups without labels.
explanations.C: Correct. Regression predicts a continuous numeric target — prices, temperatures, energy demand. Common algorithms: linear regression, gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM), neural regression heads. Evaluation uses RMSE, MAE, R² — not classification metrics.
explanations.D: RL is sequential decisions under reward.

mcqs.question: Under AI Act Art. 10, which action best addresses bias in training data for a high-risk AI system intended to score job applicants?

options.A: Ignore demographic attributes and assume the model is fair
options.B: Examine possible biases likely to affect fundamental rights, apply appropriate detection / prevention / mitigation measures, and document the choices
options.C: Publish the training data openly to all applicants
